channel.title: Nature on Reflections in the Wilderness of New Atlantis
channel.description: Recent content in Nature on Reflections in the Wilderness of New Atlantis

item.title: Epistle to Polygenism
item.description:
Intro

Human biodiversity confronts us with a problem. Among the myriad physical features of man such as skin color, muscle and fat distribution and composition, epicanthic folds, head and body shapes, hair, eye color, and even blood characteristics, it pushes one towards the verdict that we are in some way different. Yet, breeding within two “disparate” populations, producing fully fertile offspring is possible and we, more or less, are capable of communicating, interacting, and understanding the life of each other despite this unnamed difference. The problem we are confronted with is the imposition of difference, with the perceived ontological grounding of oneness. How can we be both one (a single species) and many (so visibly diverse)? An answer one often finds aligned with a paradoxically regressive strain of thinking is the conclusion of polygenism. Paradoxical since it seems to find a home among those who would ally with the bias of immediacy - such as those who call themselves Christians, or enlightenment thinkers that tend to biological reductionism. The answer they provide is accepting of the immediacy of appearance, as well as behavior, and reduces it to some immutable fact of biology, or in some cases denigrates the status of the spiritual by raising appearance and behavior to some discrete and exclusive spiritual condition. Either path is the aforementioned attitude in new skin, mentioned because of the elephant in the room: racism. More will be said on this later as it relates to racial distinctions. For now, only a footnote can be made on the racist, and often Faustian, motivations behind rejecting Out of Africa, as well as upholding the fixed character of “racial” distinctions.

One historical answer to the question of the one and many in man was polygenism, the view that different races have separate origins, whether created separately by God or evolved from different apes/hominids. The idea is quite old. For example, the Roman Emperor Julian argued that the Germani and Scythians were too different from the Ethiopians to share common ancestry. Oral traditions such as the Bambuti creation myth told of three races made from three different kinds of Clay. Biblical polygenism regularly draws on the three sons of Noah as stand in for the “races”, or stock, of Black, White, and Yellow. In the 19th century, polygenism became a scientific movement, drawing Enlightenment rationalism and empiricism into it’s supposed methodology. People like Samuel George Morton and Josiah Clark Nott claimed that cranial measurements proved races were separate species. Polygenism persisted, persisted even after Darwin’s Origin of Species, with Carl Vogt and others arguing that different races descended from different apes. These days, modern science has decisively rejected polygenism. The Out of Africa genetic evidence shows that all living humans trace to a common African population within the last 200-300 thousand years. There is no genetic or fossil support for separate origins. Polygenism seems to have failed because it mistook the superficial adaptations for a more prior difference.

As it appears, the rejection of polygenism does not dissolve the phenomenon of diversity. We are one species, one blood (especially after the time of Jesus), one recent origin. And yet we appear in such a variety. My own explorations of this topic takes the unity and diversity seriously as the starting point of a spiritual investigation. Polygenism as an Enlightenment project and mere stipulation was an enterprise dead on arrival, for mainly the same reasons that taxonomy fails at being a science. Taxonomy, which as much as it can list difference in painstaking detail, cannot arrive at the generative process by which the differences in the elements it categorizes come about. Likewise, it cannot supply us with what stands as a valid separation or not. Yet, it was not wrong to sense that the diversity we see has deep roots. What polygenism lacked was a vision of how unity could give rise to multiplicity without fracturing into separate species forever. This is where spiritual science may offer us a missing link in the fact that the diversity we observe is a result of a gradual hardening and differentiation from an original spiritual unity. The physical form we now call Homo sapiens is the ultimate consolidation of a long process that began when human souls first descended into Earthly bodies in the Lemurian epoch, passed through the more plastic, liquid forms of the Atlantean epoch, and only fully solidified in the Post-Atlantean - the very period that Out of Africa genetic studies takes as it’s given object.

Why has Out of Africa become the consensus view of modern anthropology? The reason is that the evidence for a recent African origin of Homo sapiens is now so extensive that alternative models, while interesting, have now been set as aside. This is the conclusion that I have ultimately come to. There are a few alternative theories to consider before returning to Out of Africa. The first is the Out of Australia theory. This hypothesis suggested that anatomically modern humans first emerged in Australia, based on the claims of very early archaeological sites like Lake Mungo, as well as the suggestion that some ancient Australian remains showed unique morphological features. Proponents of this theory argued that the long temporal depth of human occupation indicates a possible origin. The problem is that all living humans, including Indigenous Australians, carry the same genetic markers that trace back to Africa. The oldest Y-chromosome and mitochondrial DNA lineages are found in Africa, not Australia. Moreover, the oldest fossils of Homo sapiens are African, far predating any Australian remains. Early Australian sites indicate an early migration of modern humans out of Africa, as opposed to an independent origin. Hominin Network Theory, in recent years, proposes that the fact that Neanderthals and Denisovans interbred with modern humans, creating essentially a tangled web of interbreeding across Eurasia, suggests to us that modern humans did not have a single African origin, but rather emerged from a network of archaic populations across the Old World. This interpretation misreads evidence. Interbreeding did occur, although it was limited and one-sided. The Neanderthal and Denisovan DNA found in non-African populations amounts to only 1-6% of their genomes. The remainder is overwhelmingly derived from a single source population. Additionally, no trace of these archaic hominins exists in sub-Saharan African populations, who retain the oldest human lineages. The network model confirms that the African lineage was the dominant one, absorbing small contributions from archaic populations encountered during expansion. The multi-regional model proposed that after Homo erectus left Africa nearly 2 million years ago, populations in Africa, Europe, and Asia continued to evolve in parallel, with enough gene flow between them to keep them a single species. Modern humans, in this view, would have emerged simultaneously across the Old World from local archaic ancestors. For decades, this was the main competitor to Out of Africa. There was supporting evidence by some fossil evidence of regional anatomical continuity (such as the fact that certain facial features in East Asia seemed to persist from Homo erectus to modern populations). However, when ancient DNA became available, the multiregional model collapsed. Genetic data showed that all non-African populations carry a distinct genetic signature that emerged in Africa within the last 100,000 years and spread outward. There’s no continuity of lineages from pre-sapiens populations in Europe or Asia into modern humans. Only the small admixture noted prior. The scientific case for Out of Africa seems to be settled. But for those who ask why humans are so diverse, why this particular species expanded so rapidly, and what the meaning of our unity and diversity might be, science offers limited answers. The anthroposophic spiritual-scientific framework helps us to situate Out of Africa within a broader narrative, becoming material testament to spiritual reality.

Humans and Apes

Before continuing, it is important to note one thing. The animal kingdom (including the apes) did not arise through a linear evolution from lower to higher culminating in humans. Instead, animals represent forms separated from the human stream at various stages of Earth evolution. During the Lemurian and Atlantean Epochs, the human physical form remained relatively “plastic” and capable of being shaped by the will, while certain group souls took hold of physical bodies that had hardened prematurely, giving rise to the various animal species. Apes in particular are described as a branch that split off during the Lemurian epoch, when the human form was still soft. Their physical bodies became fixed earlier, while the human form retained its malleability, allowing it to later receive the fully incarnated human “I”. Thus, the anatomical similarities between apes and humans are not evidence of common descent in the Darwinian sense, it is a reflection of a shared origin in the plastic human form from which apes diverged. This has implications for how we interpret the fossil hominins such as australopithecines, Homo habilis, Homo erectus, and so on. They represent the various side branches or transitional forms that either preceded the full descent of the human “I” into physical bodies, or were left behind as the human form continued to refine and harden. The fact that the first stone tools appear with australopithecines shows that even before the “I” fully incarnated, beings with a degree of intelligence and will were interacting with the physical world. Steiner would likely say that these beings were ensouled by group souls (or by individual souls not yet fully independent) who were preparing the physical “vehicle” for the eventual incarnation of the fully self-conscious “I”. When we come to Homo sapiens in the Quaternary period, we see the completion of the process. The physical body is fully hardened, and the human “I” can incarnate into it with full self-consciousness/self-awareness. The Out of Africa populations can be said to represent the stock in which this final hardening and spiritual integration took place.

Physical Consolidation

The three periods we shall concern ourselves with are the Lemurian, Atlantean, and our current post-Atlantean epochs. The Middle Lemurian period, correlating roughly to the Mesozoic period, was the beginning of physical incarnation. The human body was still soft and malleable, bones were not fully mineralized, and so durable fossils did not exist. This was also following the time the sexed distinction made its emergence. The Atlantean period, correlating roughly to the Tertiary (the Paleogene and Neogene), found a progressive hardening of the world and of man. Bodies were much more liquid-like compared to today, yet the skeletal system was still not fully solid. The first durable artifacts of man appear only at the very end. It is with the Post-Atlantean period, correlating to the quaternary (Pleistoscene and Holocene), that “hardening” completed. Bones fully mineralized, the modern brain structure emerged (as evidenced by the Kalambo structure, demonstrating that even 500,000 years ago, beings on Earth were capable of planning and complex functions), and the human form as we know it today cemented itself. If Steiner’s account is accurate, we should expect the physical record to roughly correlate and show no durable evidence from the Lemurian epoch, because the body, and its lifestyle, did not leave much to be found. We should also find only sporadic, late evidence from the Atlantean epoch, as hardening gradually progressed (we will not touch the topic of inherited Atlantean wisdom in this article). Additionally, we should also find abundant evidence of fully modern humans from the post-Atlantean epoch onward. This is precisely what the fossil and archeological records reveal. During the Lemurian period, there are no accepted hominin fossils or stone tools. The few anomalous claims (such as the Eocene tools of the 19th century) have been refuted as either misinterpretations of the data or frauds. However, this does not serve as a problem, because it is exactly what one would expect if the human form has not yet reached a level of solidity capable of leaving durable remains (and based off the lifestyle of man during the Lemurian, they were much more nature attuned than we are today. The transformation/destruction of nature into tools would not occur until the Atlantean). For most of the period, there is no evidence of “man”. The first hominins appear only in the late Miocene and early Pliocene (Sahelanthropus, Orrorin, Ardipithecus), but they left no stone tools. Only at the very end of the Pliocene, around 3.3 million years ago, do we find the first undisputed stone tools at Lomekwi, Kenya. This is the point when Steiner would say hardening was approaching completion. As the Atlantean epoch gives way to the Quaternary, the first durable evidence of a being capable of thought and technology begins to appear. Finally, in the Quaternary period, with the beginning of the Pleistocene after the global flood, the picture begins to resemble what we are familiar with. We find the first fossils of Homo, the spread of stone tool industries across Africa and Eurasia, and eventually, around 300 thousand years ago, the appearance of the anatomically modern Homo sapiens. It is no coincidence that cognitive and technological sophistication became possible once the physical form had fully hardened. This, along with a myriad of other factors, such as the separation intwo sexes, made it possible for the kind of independent, discriminatory inner life we are familiar with today, including its offspring: technology.

The Out of Africa genetic evidence places the origin of all living Homo sapiens within the Post-Atlantean epoch. The mitochondrial and Y-chromosomal lineages that coalesce around 200-300 thousand years ago represent the survival of a particular population in Africa whose physical constitution had reached the modern level of mineralization. From this population all living humans have a connection. This does not mean that there were no humans before that time. It means that before the Post-Atlantean epoch, the human physical form had not yet achieved the degree of solidity that would allow it to fossilize in the kind of way we recognize today, and that the genetic lineages of those earlier forms either died out or were absorbed into the later, more hardened populations. In this way, the Out of Africa bottleneck is a genetic signature, representing the final stabilization of the modern human body.

Origin of the Races

If the human physical form only fully hardened in the Quaternary, then the diversity we see among human populations today must have arisen after that consolidation. For those that have followed thus far, this conclusion certainly makes sense. It is also consistent with the scientific view that the major physical differences among human groups are recent adaptations to different climates and environments, occurring within the last 100 thousand years or so. Steiner provides us with another, higher, aspect. The diversity we see appears to us as a matter of natural selection, or this or that theory, acting on a uniform “stock”. It may do something to that effect, but it primarily reflects the lingering influence of the spiritual forces that shaped the human body during the long epochs of hardening. The different “racial” characteristics are expressions of the different planetary and Luciferic influences that worked on the human form during the Lemurian and Atlantean epochs, only becoming fixed in the Post-Atlantean period.

In the middle of the Lemurian epoch, when the human physical form became capable of hosting the self-conscious “I”, souls began to incarnate from different planetary spheres (the beginning of physical existence, and the sexed distinction, was also the beginning of reincarnation!). This includes beings not only from Earth’s sphere, but also from Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, and other regions. These souls brought with them different spiritual-soul qualities, inclinations, temperaments, and karmic backgrounds that suited them for particular forms of earthly experience. The souls that descended from Saturn, for instance, had a natural affinity for a more inward, contemplative life; those that came from Mars brought a more active, will-oriented nature; those from Jupiter carried a certain wisdom and sense of the cosmic order. At that time, the human physical body was relatively malleable. The group souls that guided these incoming spirits worked from within to shape the body in accordance with the spiritual predisposition of each group. This is why, already in the Lemurian epoch, distinct “types” of humanity began to emerge. They would be tendencies that would later solidify into “races”.

An important factor in the formation of the races was the influence of the Luciferic beings. Luciferic spirits (particularly the aberrant Spirits of Form, also called the Luciferic Spirits of Motion) intervened in the evolution of humanity during the Lemurian and Atlantean epochs. Their goal was to accelerate the independence of the human “I” at the cost of disrupting the original unity of the human physical and etheric constitution. Had these beings not intervened, humanity would have developed from a single, uniform physical type that would have changed only slowly and in harmony with the Earth’s transformation. Instead, the Luciferic forces introduced differentiation. They hardened certain groups more quickly, fixed certain physical traits, and caused the stock of man to split into varied forms that adapted to different geographic and spiritual conditions. Steiner describes the abnormal Spirits of Form, working from the astral, taking hold of the etheric and physical bodies of different groups, imprinting them with characteristics that would later become hereditary. It’s important to clarify that these beings are not evil in the moral sense, but that their activity created the conditions for human diversity. While these also brought along with it the potential for egoism and separation, it also brought the need for the unifying force of the Christ impulse.

By the Atlantean epoch, the differentiation had crystallized into seven sub-races, from which the main racial types of the current Post-Atlantean epoch descended. At the end of Atlantis, when the continent was destroyed by the great flood (associated with the ice ages at the boundary between the Tertiary and Quaternary periods), populations migrated in different directions. According to Steiner, five main racial remnants emerged from this dispersal. I must mention importantly that these races are not to be confused with the modern day racial categories.

The first is the Black race, These are descendants of the earliest Atlantean sub-races, carrying the oldest physical forms. Their constitution is characterized by a strong astral body, making them receptive to spiritual forces in a more instinctive, dreamlike way. The next is the Yellow race, originating from the later Atlantean sub-races that migrated eastward into Asia. Their etheric body is particularly strong, giving them a higher rapport with the vital forces of nature, expressed in their languages and cultural forms. The next is the Red “American Indian” race, a branch that migrated westward into the Americas. They retained certain archaic qualities, including what Steiner calls a “memory-based consciousness”, and a close relation to the land. This red race of ancient times are not to be confused with the Native Americans of today, they represent a “declining” branch of humanity. More to be said on this later. The penultimate race is the Brown race, a mixed group which spread across the islands of Southeast Asia and the Pacific. Their physical form shows a blend of influences, and their constitution tends towards a certain dreaminess and passivity. Finally is the White race, descended from the fifth Atlantean sub-race (known as the “primal Semites”), which had its main center in what is now Ireland. This group migrated eastward across Europe and Asia, becoming the bearers of logical consciousness, the faculty that would come to full development in the Post-Atlantean epoch. Such migrations established the broad racial categories that have persisted into historical times. However, it is important to understand that the “races” we observe today are not identical to the original Atlantean forms. Over millennia, the intermixing with Homo sapiens and various other hominids, adaptation to new environments, and the ongoing process of soul incarnation have continuously reshaped these populations. The categories and characteristics that seem to hold so much weight today are in fact an outer residue of a process which began in the Lemurian period and is now gradually coming to the end.

As regarding the modern “races”, these five are definitely not “pure” types; they have intermingled throughout history well into our current Post-Atlantean period. The boundaries are fluid, and the characteristics attributed to the archaic races are general tendencies, not static absolutes. Migration and population is a very complex thing, involving moving over time, backflows of migration (creating a recursive situation), and internal differentiation within a given population. Throughout time, a tendency towards the flourishing of the individual has emerged, helped by the fact that Steiner emphasizes that these racial differences are not permanent. In the future, as humanity evolves toward spiritual unity, the concept of race will lose its meaning. For more on this, I have written two articles: The Problem of Africa in German Idealism and Anthroposophy’s Treatment as a Response to Afro-Pessimism and Post-Scripts to the Racialism in Anthroposophy

Each of the five main races is associated with a specific geographical region from which certain cosmic forces ebb and flow. Steiner indicates that there are five centers on Earth - in Africa, Asia, the Americas, and elsewhere - where the etheric forces that shape racial characteristics are concentrated. These centers are points through which planetary influences (the same planets that hosted the descending souls) work on the human constitution. The Lemurian and Atlantean epochs were the periods of race formation, a time when the physical and etheric bodies of man were molded into distinct types under the influence of spiritual beings. The Post-Atlantean epoch, by contrast, is the period of soul development. The task for this epoch is not to create new races (although one trip to New York and you will find combinations never before conceived, Yemeni-Puerto Ricans and Bengali-Russians alike). It is a time for the cultivation of the individual “I”, enabling man to overcome the limitations of heredity and race. This is why, in the Post-Atlantean epoch, the concept of race loses its significance. The five main races, therefore, are remnants of an earlier stage of evolution. Their continued existence serves a purpose only insofar as they provide a variety of physical and etheric conditions through which the soul can gain a kind of training, but the direction of evolution is towards unity. Like a teacher who has finished teaching a student erases their vocation, leaving behind a learned pupil, so too shall the racial conditions serve their purpose for the development of man.

Adam and Eve Revisited: One and Too Many

What is left after the initially unified “man” having been a purely spiritual form, then concretized and materialized into the many kinds of man, is revealed to be a transitionary stage towards a reunion of unity? We may bring to the fore the archetypes which underlie the unity of man: Adam and Eve. It is quite fitting that modern science has taken the symbolic archetypes found in the bible in its monikers of “Y-chromosomal Adam” and “Mitochondrial Eve”. The familiar biblical account of Adam and Eve has long been read as the story of two individual human beings, the first man and woman from whom all humanity descended. Modern genetics has shown that this literal interpretation cannot stand. There was no “first man” or “first woman” in the sense of a solitary breeding pair. Yet the truth of the narrative, properly understood illuminates the findings of the mineralistic sciences. We will re-appropriate these figures in order to help us understand them as the primal polarity of group spirit and group soul, and check the unending modulation of the one and many.

The two figures are not physical individuals. They represent the group spirit/soul of nascent humanity. Adam (“the one taken from the earth”) stands for the unified “I-being” of humanity, or in other words, the collective spiritual identity from which all individual human spirits descend. Eve (“the animate one”, or “the mother of all living”) represents the group soul, the diversifying principle that gives form to the physical, etheric, and astral bodies of human beings across the Earth. Spiritually, we are one. All human souls share the same origin in the Adam-I. Physically, we are many. The Eve-soul differentiates itself into the varied forms and temperaments that allow souls to undergo the experience and training necessary for their evolution.

The more esoteric connection between the names Yahweh and Eve goes even further. Yahweh, the “Earth Lord”, is the being who guides the development of the human “I” on Earth. The name Yahweh contains the Yodh, which represents the individual I-being, that glimmer of selfhood that descends into the human soul. Eve, as the “Earth Mother” embodies the accumulated forces of the Old Saturn, Old Sun, and Old Moon stages of Earth evolution, forces which build the physical, etheric, and astral bodies that serve as a dwelling place for the “I”. Thus in the name “Yahweh”, we have the union of the spiritual I (the Adam principle), with it’s concretized form (the Eve principle). This union is the true “first couple”, the foundational polarity of spirit and soul that makes human existence on Earth possible. With this in mind, the apparent contradiction between man’s genetic unity and physical diversity dissolves. The one is the Adam-I, the unified spiritual origin of all human beings. Every human soul, regardless of race or epoch, participates in the same I-being. This is partly why, despite all the outward differences, we can recognize one another as fellow humans, why the deepest spiritual development always leads to the experience of a universal brotherhood. The differences end up negligible. The “many” is the Eve-soul, the variable manifest as the variety of physical forms, temperaments, and cultural expressions. The Eve-soul is a collective soul that “gives birth” to the multiplicity of human forms. Hence, from a single spiritual stock, diversity in man can come about.

Through the development of the “I”, and reception of the Christ impulse, individuals can increasingly free themselves from the constraints of heredity and race. The Pauline saying, that “There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is neither male nor female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus” makes this goal express. The reunification of the many into one, without the loss of individuality, is a foregone conclusion.

item.title: Man, Animal, and Evolution: The Relationship Finally Clarified
item.description:
Intro

After writing my recent article Epistle to Polygenism, a few insights came to mind regarding the human form and it’s arrangement, going hand in hand with an elaboration of the relationship between man and animals. This article is on this consideration. In this undertaking, the process describing the generation of embodied form these beings take is necessary, namely, an explication of “evolution”, which is the name for this process. In its conventional usage, “evolution” refers generally to the gradual physical transformation of species over geological time, driven by variations and natural selection (which is a catch-all term employed to describe a failure for an organism to reach biotic potential). This framework has provided the world with a few insights, surely, yet it leaves more basic questions unanswered. Why did self-consciousness emerge only in one lineage? What is the inner relationship between the human form and the animal forms that so closely resemble it? Why does the fossil record show long periods of status punctuated by sudden appearances, rather than the smooth continuum the theory might predict?

To address these, we must expand our understanding of evolution to include dimensions that the physical sciences, by their very methodology, cannot access. Spiritual-scientific research offers precisely such an expansion. In this account, evolution is not merely the history of bodily forms. It involves the evolution of the planet, of spirits, or the ideas and concepts by which perceptions are generated by, the whole of nature, and so on and so forth. Thus far, the only theory in materialistic sciences approaching such a conception is the Expanding Earth hypothesis, which can explain everything modern plate tectonics does except for not taking as an assumption the fixed radius of the Earth over time. In addition, it also answers questions such as the relatively young age of the ocean floor, and a few others. Besides this theory, modern materialist science treats nature as dead, and if not dead, then it is dead at the moment of observation, put into hypostasis for the thinking subject. Evolution as properly understood involves the spiritual hierarchies who guide cosmic and earthly development, dimensions of evolution such as Conditions of Life, Form, and Consciousness, planetary stages, and involution and sacrifice wherein higher spiritual beings descend into lower states to create conditions for humanity’s ascent. Evolution can be said to be sustained by a cosmic sacrifice. Within this framework, the relationship between humanity and the natural kingdoms, for instance, does not appear to us as one of linear emergence. It is a stratified separation. Telos is also involved. If something is developing, there is something it develops towards, and so the spiritual science’s understanding of evolution does not end with the present, nor does it end with biological organisms. Everything in nature is alive, as Schelling states. However, this will be elaborated on at a further date. The question concerning us today is along the following: Why do we look like apes?

Monkey

Humans and great apes have a strikingly similar skeletal structure. The arrangement of limbs, the opposable thumbs, the facial musculature, and so on, have long been held up as the crowning evidence for Darwinian evolution. When Huxley, Darin’s “bulldog”, famously toured the British Isles with a chimpanzee skeleton, his message was very clear. The human body is merely a modified ape body according to him. The resemblance is so close that comparative anatomy leaves little room for doubt. We share a common ancestor, the ape form is our past, and the human form is a more refined variation of the same basic blueprint, problem solved. This argument has become the bedrock of modern evolutionary biology.

Note: The entire field of evolution tends to be pseudo-science, lacking all forms of explanatory value or normativity. Whether evolutionary psychology or biology, it does not matter. What a trait in one organism is said to be an adaptation, the respective opposite trait in another is also said to be a beneficial adaptation. For instance, a dull color in a bird may be beneficial in order for it to survive and reach biotic potential (reproduce). Within the same species bright-colored feathers is explained away as beneficial for attracting mates and reaching biotic potential. If any trait can be rationalized post hoc, its explanatory power becomes null. More on this: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Just-so_story. While the counterargument to this statement is that an emphasis on different selective pressures resulting in fitness is the main point might seem attractive, further probing reveals that fitness (survival and reproduction) becomes a tautological loop of reproduction. “What reproduces is more fit, and the fittest survive” becomes “those reproduce more reproduce more”. Evolutionary psychology is similarly a fruitless domain parading itself as normative theory.

The resemblance of monkey to man is elegant, parsimonious, and seemingly supported by a wealth of genetic and fossil data. Yet for all its explanatory power, it still rests on the assumption that morphological similarity necessarily implies genealogical descent. In other words, because we look like apes, we must have evolved from them. This is an unjustified assumption that will find no solution in the materialistic sciences. Modern neo-Darwinian synthesis is ultimately a theory of form. It explains how populations adapt to environments through variation and natural selection, supposedly. There are disagreements, but we can grant this for the sake of argument. We may also grant that it successfully accounts for the diversification of species, the distribution of traits, and the patterns of genetic relatedness. The human-ape similarity fits into this framework, that we share a primate body because we inherited it from a primate ancestor, with modifications that accumulated along separated linages. However, the limit is reached precisely at the threshold of what makes us human. The aforementioned theory cannot explain why, among all primates, only one lineage developed self-consciousness, the capacity for abstract thought, moral reflection, language, art, and so on. It treats these as emergent epiphenomena of increased brain size, yet it has no account of why the brain expanded in this lineage alone, or how mere quantity of neurons could ever produce the qualitative leap to the reflexive “I”. The evolutionary biologist is left with what Thomas Nagel coined “the hard problem” of consciousness. This is a problem that neo-Darwinian synthesis and natural selection was never equipped to solve.

Additionally, Darwinism (as shorthand) treats the animal kingdom as a series of steps leading *coincidentally to man. The ape is positioned as our nearest relative, a kind of unfinished version of ourselves. This narrative implicitly carries a hierarchy, but it cannot ground that hierarchy in anything beyond differential reproductive success. Morality, art, philosophy, and language all become byproducts of a brain that happened to get larger, with an unclear object in mind, and in some cases denies the object is there at all. The human form is reduced to a contingent outcome of mutation and selection, with no intrinsic meaning or direction.

Spiritual science offers a fundamentally different reading of the same anatomical facts. In this view, the human being is the original form. Not in the sense of a physical ancestor, but as an archetypal spiritual reality that preceded the physical separation of the kingdoms of nature. Long ago, during periods of times named the “Lemurian” and “Atlantean” epochs, the human physical body was still malleable and had not yet solidified into the fixed skeletal form we know today. At various stages of this long process of solidification, certain beings that guide species from the astral plane (called group souls) took hold of physical bodies that had prematurely hardened. These bodies, which had once been part of the human stream, became the foundations for the animal kingdom. The apes represent the last such separation, occurring when the human form was already quite close to its final shape. This is why their anatomy so closely resembles ours. They are a kind of photograph of the human form at the moment it branched away. In this conception, a portion of developing humanity “lags behind” and becomes the basis for a new natural kingdom. The animals, plants, and minerals are not our ancestors; they are our own former possibilities frozen in time.

The ape, therefore, is not a primitive ancestor, but a side branch. It is a being that took on the physical form that the human stream had at a particular stage, but then stopped developing further. The anatomical similarity cannot be evidence that we evolved from apes, it is instead evidence that apes and humans share a common origin in the plastic proto-human form which existed before the final hardening of the human body.

If this reading is correct, then the fossil hominins (australopithecines, Homo habilis, Homo erectus, and even earlier forms) take on a different significance, representing various side branches or transitional forms that either preceded the full descent of the human “I” into physical bodies, or were left behind as the human form continued to refine and harden. As mentioned in my Polygenism article, the fact that stone tools appear with australopithecines shows that even before the “I” fully incarnated, beings with a degree of intelligence and will were interacting with the physical world. These beings, in Steiner’s account, were ensouled by group souls (or by individual souls not yet fully independent) who were preparing the physical “body” for the eventual incarnation of the self-conscious, reflexive “I”. The appearance of anatomically modern Homo sapiens in the Quaternary period (precisely when the Out of Africa population emerges) marks the completion of the hardening process. The physical body is stably mineralized and capable of hosting the “I”.

Conventional evolutionary theory, exactly because it limits itself to physical form and a vague notion of genetic inheritance, cannot accommodate the above picture. It has no language for telos, ideality, freedom, self-determination, etc. Yet these are precisely the dimensions needed to answer the questions that Darwinism leaves open. Why does consciousness exist? Why only in humans? Ultimately, what is the relationship between the human form and the animal form that surrounds us? Spiritual science certainly does not reject the empirical findings of paleontology, genetics, taxonomy studies, or comparative anatomy. Instead, it offers a broader view for these findings, one that interprets the same data in a fuller way and recontextualizes the information accordingly in a unified way. The ape-human similarity then transforms from an animal origin to a human origin.

Influence and Development

In order to understand the human-animal relationship, let us set aside the image of evolution as a linear chain from simple to complex, of ape to man. In its place, we must envision a process. Rationalize this process however you want; the Hegelian abstract to concrete, the alchemical process, separation to hardening to inward transformation, etc. are ways to understand the procedural moments here. This is a process that spans beyond the history of the Earth as we currently understand it into the period of time known as the middle Lemurian epoch (roughly 359–299 million years ago). If the “archetype” of man was abstract (spiritual, so to say) at some point, then this is the period it began to “harden” and manifest a body in the world for itself (in addition to the beginning of the sex distinction, among others). The human form was not yet the fixed structure we know today. Steiner describes it as “plastic” often, “soft”, and still directly shaped by the will. Bones had not yet fully mineralized, the body was more fluid, more responsive to the inner life of the soul, as a necessary condition for the incarnation of the human “I” in the world. During this epoch, the human being moved through the world with a body that was, in essence, a sculpture in process. The forces that would later become the distinct animal, plant, and mineral kingdoms were still contained within the human form. The human being was, as Steiner puts it, a kind of “potential totality” from which all other terrestrial forms would eventually be separated. It is worth noting that the Earth and man concurrently “evolve”, however, we are focused on man and animals for now.

The Atlantean epoch continued this process. The body remained more fluid than today. For example the skeletal system was still not fully solid. Yet a progressive hardening was underway. The first really durable artifacts appear only at the very end of Atlantis, marking the threshold where human will began to encounter a world that was itself becoming mineralized. The great disaster and flood that ended Atlantis finalized this transition. The human form, now sufficiently hardened, could enter the current post-Atlantean epoch as a stable vessel for the fully incarnated “I”. Why did the human form need to harden exactly? Because the “I” requires a respective stable instrument. A body that is too plastic cannot reliably support the kind of self-conscious reflection that defines true humanity. The hardening of the skeleton, the mineralization of the bones, the stabilization of the brain structure, etc. are the invented culmination of a long spiritual preparation. The fossil record, when read this way, reflects this gradual solidification. The reflexive, self-conscious human “I” did not always dwell in a physical body though. During earlier planetary stages of Earth’s development, the human soul moved among the spiritual hierarchies, gradually preparing for embodiment. The descent into a physical form in one sense is considered a sacrificial act. The higher “I” bound itself to a body that would limit it, in order for it to gain the capacity for freedom, self-knowledge, love, etc. This descent did not happen all at once. In the Lemurian epoch, the “I” began to inhabit the still plastic human form, but was not yet fully integrated. During the Atlantean epoch, the “I” deepened its connection, but still the body was not entirely stable. Only in our epoch did the “I” achieve full-self consciousness within a fully hardened body.

This brings us to today. If the animal kingdom consists of forms that separated from the human stream at various stages, then the human being accurately is more than the synthesis of animal traits as a being who inwardly transformed what animals externalized. This is the principle of metamorphosis which Goethe recognized in plants where leaf transforms into sepal, petal, stamen, etc., and in animals, where the vertebrae transformed into skull bones. This extends into the whole of evolution. The human form is a metamorphosed unity of what appears in the animal kingdom as separate, specialized organs.

Consider the fact that what in animals is an external organ becomes an internal organ in humans. The fish’s swimming bladder, which regulates buoyancy, is metamorphosed into the human eardrum. The fish’s gills become the tiny bones of the ear. What was once spread out in water becomes concentrated and internalized. What in animals is a specialized function becomes a unified capacity in humans. Steiner talks in depth on the siphonophore, a colony of distinct individuals each performing a single function such as balance, feeding, movement, reproduction, or defense. This is a kind of deconstructed human. In the human being, all these functions are drawn together into a single self-conscious organism, held together by the “I”. When the “I” fails to hold them together, we see the fragmentations of mental illness, a kind of return, in distorted form, to the condition of the siphonophore. One may also consider that a one-sided development becomes a balanced threefoldness in humans. Animals are, as Steiner says, “human beings which have remained stationary in the one-sided development of their characteristics”. The ant has developed social organization to an extreme, but lacks individuality. The beaver has developed engineering wisdom, but cannot innovate. The human being, in contrast, holds thinking, feeling, and willing, in a balance that requires the insecurity and openness that the one-sided animal lacks.

This inward transformation certainly didn’t happen automatically. It was guided by spiritual beings working from the astral plane. Since animals don’t have an “I”, they are guided by animal group-souls, beings on the astral plane who direct the collective intelligence of the species (think the beaver’s dam, the bird’s nest or migration, and the bee’s hive). The third elementary Kingdom works directly on animal forms, shaping them according to instincts and desires. In the human being, these formative forces are internalized. The same forces that shape the animal from without become, in the human, the inner forces of conscience and self-direction. In other words, the human “I” learns to do consciously what the group soul does instinctively.

The ape occupies a special place in this picture. It separated from the human stream last, when the human form was already close to its final shape. Its anatomy is therefore most similar to ours for this reason, because it froze at the stage just before the human form achieved its full uprightness, the capacity to receive the “I”, and so on. Moreover, Steiner notes that the ape “brings too much from the group soul down into its own individual existence”. Unlike lower animals, which fully return to the group soul at death, the ape retains something in its physical organization that cannot go back. This gives it a quality that appears almost individual- a something that resembles the human “I”, but is in fact a stalled potentiality. This residue becomes, in the spiritual world, the elementals called salamanders, beings that are neither fully human, nor fully animal, lingering as a kind of unfinished creation.

Everything is for a purpose or, the form of man

The animal kingdom consisting of potentials separated from man is on the one hand. The other hand focuses on what the animal kingdom is a derivation of: the human form. Then human form can thus be understood as the living synthesis of these potentials, more than the sum of the parts. It contains these potentials by having turned them inside out, under the sovereignty of the “I”. Among all features that distinguish the human body, three stand out as especially revealing. First is the upright posture, then the articulate larynx, and finally, the free hand with its opposable thumb. Each of these is a metamorphosed animal organ, an inward transformation of what in the animal kingdom appears as an external structure.

The most visible difference between humans and the higher animals is the vertical orientation of the spine. In the animal, the spine runs horizontally. The head is attached at an angle, its weight being carried by the forelimbs. In the human being, the spine stands upright, and the head balances freely atop it. For conventional biology, this is a simple and mere addendum to bipedalism, one that freed the hands for tool use. For spiritual science, that which subsumes the material sciences, it is the physical expression of the “I”. Steiner explains that the upright posture serves as a necessary condition for self-consciousness, in addition to it being a mechanical advantage. When the spine is horizontal, the astral body flows predominantly in the direction of the Earth’s surface, anchoring consciousness in instinct. But when the spine becomes vertical, the astral body can orient itself toward the cosmos, allowing the “I” to descend into the physical body. The upright human being stands as a column connecting heaven and earth, as a microcosmic counterpart to the cosmic order.

The uprightness is also the basis for the threefold division of the human organism into the nervous system, rhythmic system, and metabolic limb-system mentioned in a previous article on the development of human physiology and spirituality. Each of these three systems have their own relation to the animal kingdom, meaning in the animal, these functions are often spatially separated into distinct organs, or even distinct creatures. In the human being, they are drawn together into a single upright form, integrated by the “I”. The upright posture makes possible a second transformation via the metamorphosis of the air-organism into the organ of speech.

Consider a bird. Its entire organization is oriented toward the air, with its hollow bones, its lungs connected to air sacs extending throughout the body. The bird’s song is produced by a syrinx that vibrates with the breath. It is, in a way, an instrument of the air elementals, being shaped by the element it moves through. The song can be considered an instinctive yet direct expression of the group soul. During the Lemurian epoch, when the human form was still plastic, one possible evolutionary path was to become a bird-like being. Had the human stream talent that direction, we would have become creatures of the air, our whole organization lightened and specialized for flight, and our consciousness, merged with the astral currents of the atmosphere. But that path was not taken. Instead, the human being concentrated the air element into the organ of the larynx.

What in the bird is spread throughout the whole human body as the air sacs, pneumatic skeleton, and functional song, is in the human being inwardly concentrated. The larynx is, as Steiner describes, an inverted or “transformed” bird, it is an inverted air organ. It is the place where the air is shaped, and where breath becomes articulated sound, and subsequently where that sound becomes another instrument of the “I”. This inversion explains why the upright posture is so intimately connected with speech. Only when the spine is vertical and the head is balanced can the larynx develop the flexibility necessary for articulate language. The ape, which walks only partially upright, has a larynx that remains a call organ. Capable of signalling emotion, surely, but incapable of the nuanced articulation that goes hand in hand with thought.

The last metamorphosis we will concern ourselves with is the hand. In animals, the forelimb is specialized for a single function. The hoof is used for running, the wing for flight, the paw for grasping, the fin for swimming, and so on. Each is a perfect adaptation to a specific environment, but each is also one-sided. In the human being, the forelimb is notably unspecialized, it is the universal organ that can become a toolmaker, a painter, a gesture of blessing a caress, and similarly a strike, an insult, a warrior, etc. The universality here is a result of a double metamorphosis. Steiner explains that the human hand is not simply an unmodified animal paw or this or that, recall that the human archetype takes precedence. The specialized forelimbs are in actuality the one-sided determinations of the human hand. It is in reality the inward integration of what in the animal kingdom is distributed across different forms. The strength of the hoof, dexterity of the paw, lightness of the wing- these are all potentials concentrated into the hand. The human hand is free because it has not been forced into any single function. It can learn any function because it is guided by the “I”. The opposable thumb is the physical sign of this freedom. In the animal, the digits are often locked into a fixed pattern. In the human, they are arranged to meet one another, to create a circuit of touch and manipulation that unironically mirrors the circuit of self-consciousness.

Evolution is a constant process of turning structures inside out, and concentrating and diluting functions. “Human” is thus the category of being which holds as its characteristic positionality the metamorphosed unity of all animal potentials. As Goethe intuited, the human being is the “sum” of the animal kingdom, although this should not be read literally. It is through a transformation of each animal quality into a higher human faculty, and a gradual falling away and one-sidedness of these same qualities in man that we should view the animal kingdom in relation to us. We should come to re-cognize the lion’s courage as moral strength, the eagle’s vision as piercing insight, and the serpent’s cunning as wisdom.

Possible forms, roads untaken, and form clarified

Mentioned previously was how we could have taken an evolutionary path resulting in a bird-like form. As an avid lover of sci-fi, a question arises for me. What other forms could have emerged? The body we inhabit today is the successful result of a process of differentiation and avoidance. Guided by higher spiritual being, the human stream was consciously sculpted from many potential animal-like outcomes. Each of these potentials, had it had been allowed to harden, would have procured a creature as distinct from us as the ape is, yet each would have carried within it a parietal reflection of what the human being was meant to become. These alternative forms remain in their separated kingdoms, as reminds of the choices embedded in the process of evolution. Steiner explicitly mentions several such potentialities.

In the early Lemurian epoch, before the head had fully differentiated as the organ of self-consciousness, the human form was structured very differently, that is to say that the metabolic-limb system was dominant. The head, by contrast, was relatively undeveloped, more like an appendage than any sort of sovereign centrality. If this configuration had hardened, the result would have been a being of immense physical strength but dull consciousness; a giant. This potential is associated with the cyclopean beings of old, creatures of immense power who lacked the refinement of individual thought, with their singular eye representing a symbol of a unified and undifferentiated consciousness. This potential was avoided through the development of the larynx and the eventual specialization of the nervous system, centered in the head. The human being, instead of becoming a brute giant, concentrated the forces of will and movement into the limbs, while developing the head as the organ of self-reflection. The giant today remains a memory of what could have been.

At another stage, a different influence predominated. Beings associated with the air element (sylphs and their spiritual guides) worked strongly on the human form, offering a potential path toward the description previous of lightness and flight. Had the human stream taken this direction, our whole organization would have become quite pneumatic. We would have hollow bones, air sacs throughout the body, sing rather than speak, and have an instinctive and dispersed consciousness, fully merged with the group soul. We would have become beings of air. This potential was separated off and became the bird kingdom. The human being did not become a bird, as you can tell. Instead, the air element was concentrated into the aforementioned larynx, resulting in a spiritualized version of the air potential.

Another major potential is the one that concerns us most directly, since it produced the mammals, and among them, the ape! At the stage when the human form was already approaching its final shape, a hardening occurred. Certain beings (influenced by the Luciferic and Ahrimanic forces) could not wait for the full transformation. Their physical organization solidified prematurely, producing a form that mimics the human structure in a fixed, specialized way. Why did the ape not complete the journey? Precisely because of the temptation of Luciferic and Ahrimanic beings. During the Lemurian epoch, Luciferic beings aimed to bestow self-awareness prematurely, while Ahrimanic beings aimed to harden and calcify form before the “I” was ready to descend. The poor ape was grabbed these forces and solidified at the threshold. Its body reflects a state of a soul that is *trapped. Imitative and cunning, but tied to the physical-etheric world, carrying within it the outlines of an individuality never attained. This is why the ape has always held a strange fascination. When one gazes upon the face of an ape, they witness trapped intelligence.

The human form was guided away from these potentials by the hierarchy of spiritual beings working to consciously sculpt the human form. The Lords of Form (Exusiai) held the human archetype in the spiritual worlds, preventing it from solidifying prematurely. The Spirits of Motion (Dynamis) directed the formative forces away from one-sided developments. Even the human “I”, before its full descent, exerted a longing for a form that could house self-consciousness. The result is a body that is uncompromising in its need for freedom. The animal kingdom, likewise, is the family of forms that could have been us, had we not been guided toward something higher.

Implications and the future

The relationship posed before us has implications that extends beyond the past and into the future. Mention was made of the evolution of the Earth, matter, nature (including physics), and spiritual beings, however, we will still focus on man and animal. But it is worth noting that these will change in the future as well.

The principle of animal forms arising as separated potentials does not end with the Lemurian Atlantean, or current post-Atlantean epoch. It is a recurring dynamic, one that will be simultaneous with the evolution of humanity through successive planetary stages. The Earth is destined to undergo further transformations, with the next being called “Jupiter”, “Venus”, and then “Vulcan”. At each stage, the category of being “human”, one who is the central being of Earth evolution, will develop new faculties, and new kingdoms will branch off from the human stream. For example on the future Jupiter condition, Steiner indicates that what is currently the animal kingdom will have evolved into a kind of intermediate kingdom, reflection the separated potential of a humanity with a highly realized “I”. Meanwhile, new animal-like beings will separate from humanity. These will be beings who, during our current stage of Earth evolution, did not fully integrate the “I” into their physical and etheric bodies. The group souls that now ensoul animals will have advanced, while some human souls (or perhaps aspects of human souls) will devolve into group-soul existence, forming new natural kingdoms. Ponder this: the fact that supersensible human faculties will be developed, and that the principle of animal engendering is still occurring, suggests to us an animal kingdom in the future that will reflect an animal kingdom comprising the potentiality of supersensible perception. Psychic-apes residing in an intermediate stage of “man” and “animal” is not a discounted possibility in the future.

We may also take into consideration that the extinction of a species is not only an ecological event. It may play a part, but it is largely a reflection, and sometimes a consequence of, the general spiritual posture of humanity. When a species disappears, its group soul withdraws from physical manifestation. In some cases, this withdrawal is part of the natural rhythm of evolution. Maybe the group soul has completed its task and moves on to new forms. In other cases, extinction is accelerated, or even caused by human activity. This carries spiritual significance.

Take the passenger pigeon, which was once the most numerous bird in North America, driven to extinction in the early 20th century by industrial scale hunting and habitat destruction. Or the great auk, the thylacine, or the great American buffalo (which is not extinct just yet!), or any of the countless species lost in the wake of human expansion. From the perspective of spiritual science, these extinctions reflect a spiritual posture that treats the natural world as a standing reserve, one that severs the human being from the kingdoms that were once contained within him.

When a species goes extinct, its group soul does not perish. It does however withdrawal from the physical plane, and continues in the spiritual world, sometimes waiting for a future epoch when conditions are right for re-embodiment. But the manner of its withdrawal is shaped by humanity’s relationship to it. A species treated with indifference or hatred carries those qualities into its spiritual afterlife. A species that is honored and loved leaves behind a different seedling. This places a huge responsibility on humanity. Extinction defined as the loss of biodiversity is a bankrupt definition. The true meaning is that it is a spiritual event which reflects our own inner state. When allowing a species to vanish, we are, in a sense, amputating part of ourselves, that part of ourselves which is a potential that we have refused to recognize or redeem. The current wave of extinctions driven by human activity can be considered a mirror, showing us a humanity that has forgotten kinship with the natural world and hardened itself against the very kingdoms that once made its own evolution possible.

Now, one might object that if animals are only “separated potentials”, why do we see a clear progression from simpler to more complex forms in the fossil record? Why do later animals appear to possess more developed consciousness (sentience) than earlier ones? The direction of the process is important here. The materialist standpoint sees complexity and apparent consciousness as proof of evolution’s power, as if the process itself were gradually producing mind. From the spiritual scientific perspective, which encompasses the materialistic sciences and includes philosophy (hence the grounding of any possible deductive knowledge), the direction is reversed. Matter is the sediment of consciousness, and the increasing complexity of animal forms over geological time is the vestigial remnants of a gradual revelation of potentials that were always present in the spiritual world.

When we see a modern animal with complex social behavior or problem-solving skills, we’re witnessing the descending arc of group-soul consciousness, which was always there, now becoming more visible as the physical forms that expresses it become higher in definition. The animal does not approach humanity, rather, the group soul, over vast periods, expresses itself through forms that are increasingly capable of reflecting its wisdom. Materialist evolution, lacking this framework, will naturally interpret the same facts as evidence of a continuous and blind upward-driving process. It will bring up dolphins, the empathy of elephants, crows which solve problems, monkey which use grunts and body language to communicate, and so on as proof that consciousness is an emergent property of complex nervous systems. But this interpretation mistakes the how for the why. Still, there are those who would, in the future, see the new kingdoms engendered by man as proof of evolution. If they still believe in materialist nonsense by then, they will be relegated to the 8th sphere, becoming the animals they so desperately want to see as approaching humanity.

The human form in the future will also evolve and metamorphose further. I will bring up the larynx just one more time. This organ, which currently serves speech and song, is destined for a further transformation. Steiner describes that on the future Jupiter condition (a stage of Earth’s evolution), the human larynx will evolve into an organ of generation/creation in a much higher sense than it currently is. The word will become directly creative, as it was in olden times, only now unified. What is now spoken will then be, carrying with it the power of actualization. This future capacity will not be automatically given to all, however. Those human beings who do not properly develop the necessary faculties, such as misusing speech and hardening their hearts, will have this potential “hardened” or “split off”. They will become the seed for a new kingdom, just as the apes and other animals arose from those who could not wait for the full descent of the “I”. Yet again, future animal kingdoms will be crystallized human potentials.

Evolution is presented to us as a spiral. What happened in the past on the physical level will happen in the future on a more spiritual level. The animals of the future will not be identical to today’s animals, but they will be externalized group soul expressions of human capacities that were not individually developed. Likewise, we have before us an ethical responsibility. By developing our higher faculties, especially the “I” in freedom and love, we are also preventing the unnecessary fragmentation of the human form into new separated kingdoms. Conversely, what we neglect or pervert (or refuse to transform) can become, in future ages, a new animal-like race. The choices we make today are shaping the forms that will surround our posterity beyond.

item.title: The Development of Human Physiology and Spirituality
item.description:
Note: This is a first draft of this article, probably [not] to be updated in the future. As a result, some of the writing is not up to par, repetitive, unclear. For now, it is informative enough to post.

Intro

Human development is among the first order concerns of humanity. After all, self-knowledge is the beginning of all true knowledge, regardless of if this self is a broad and abstract notion of spirit/source, or if this “self” is the immediate ground by which the possibilities for the conditions of freedom may realize themselves. As it follows then, a brief and high level elementary note on what “man”, or “humanity”, is constituted by is necessary. Man is ultimately a category of being which is capable of self-determination. As a consequence, we are defined much less by what we are than what we can become. It’s quite a paradoxical position we find ourselves in, since in order to realize freedom and determine ourselves, certain conditions need to be in place. Namely, the possibility of failure, non-being or a gradual falling-away from the kind of category we are (which comes hand in hand with failure. It is entirely possible to do deeds which would eventually have us ceasing to be human), and most importantly, a concretization of this being in the world. These conditions demonstrate for us the fact that freedom is not given, just as the saying goes: you can lead a horse to water, but you cannot make it drink. Just as the horse needs to want the water in order for it to drink, freedom needs to be realized by the soul of the individual as a free act wholeheartedly, otherwise it is not freedom.

The capacity for freedom does not appear fully formed. In the aforementioned conditions, the unfolding of this category of being through time, such as organic, mechanic, chemical, and spiritual structures, are of key importance to actualize the decisions man makes. The precise workings and detailing of this process will occupy us subsequently, but first we must establish the sobering fact that the complex organism “man” has many perspectives by which we may view ourselves. Body, soul, and spirit are all interrelated, and likewise the temptation must be resisted to view ourselves through a singular lens. The life and development of each and every person is contrapuntal; each narrative framing of an individual is equally true as the next, forming a totality. The same can be said for the abstract sense of development to be described. Further, the human being reveals itself only through multiple, overlapping perspectives. It is through this framework that we can begin to comprehend ourselves.

Were it not for the need to make itself real in a specific kind of way, the human being would never have achieved the conditions of freedom. In other words, if the human being were to remain purely spiritual, the exposure to external impulses and influences that sway them from a realized self-determination, and likewise freedom would not be realized. As a result, the human needs an environment to both give them the opportunity to fail (be swayed by externality), and provide some kind of conditionality which acclimates it to this externality while preserving the possibility for freedom. It is necessary that the human being is posited into nature and fashions itself a body with help from the higher spirits. More will be said in another article on this topic.

Threefold Man (Or is it four?)

Throughout various cultures and history, the differing perspectives on the totality of man have a consistent through line. Each schema emphasizes a different dimension of the same human being. For instance, the trichotomy of body, soul, and spirit is the most ancient and widespread. It makes note of the difference of the physical side, the inner life of feeling and consciousness (soul), and the eternal, self‑conscious ideality (spirit). This is the broadest division. In Plato’s Phaedrus, the soul is depicted as a charioteer driving two horses, a division that echoes into Neoplatonism and early Christian theology, where Paul’s distinction of “spirit, soul, and body” became the foundation for later trichotomist thought. When Steiner speaks of a threefold functional system (to be clarified later), he is extending this old intuition into a physiological and spiritual scientific language, carefully distinguishing the living organism from the metaphysical. The fourfold enumeration finds its classic expression in the Pythagorean Tetractys. For those unfamiliar, the Tetractys is the sacred decad (meaning 10. It is 10 because 10 contains within it the enumerations of 4: 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 10) arranged as a triangle of ten points, symbolizing the four elements, the fourfold structure of number, and the cosmos itself. Much can be said on the Tetractys. In Jewish Kabbalah, the four worlds (Atziluth, Beriah, Yetzirah, Assiah) describe a layered descent from spirit to matter, analogous to the fourfold human constitution of physical, etheric, astral, and “I.” Steiner explicitly references the Tetractys as a prefiguring of his own fourfold model. Sevenfold schemas permeate world traditions. We have the seven chakras of yogic philosophy, the seven metals of alchemy, the seven days of creation in Genesis, and the seven planets of antiquity all point to a structuring of the cosmos and the human being into seven interrelated and interpenetrating levels, to name a few. Ninefold enumerations appear most notably in the Christian angelic hierarchies, for instance where Dionysius the Areopagite arranged the nine choirs of angels into three triads. Similarly, the ninefold Enneagram, though modern, draws on ancient roots of threefold and sevenfold patterns. For Steiner, the ninefold view emerges when we distinguish the soul’s three distinct activities, each of which can be progressively transformed by the “I.” Each of these numbers - whether three, four, seven, nine - represents a different lens through which the various spiritual traditions of man have glimpsed the same object. I’d hate to sound like a broken record, but much more can be said on this, and on the totality of this development. Among these lenses, one of the more comprehensive ways of understanding the living human being is as so: firstly a description of the more functional natural distinctions which condition freedom, then how exactly they encourage a development of the threefold soul and I, and finally, a few notes on what I call the “rhythms of life”. This will present us with a schema that unveils how the human being expresses thinking, feeling, and willing in a much more concrete way than the enumerations above. Please keep in mind that this is not comprehensive, as it does not take into account things like the relationship man has to these components during incarnated life vs. the time from death and new birth, the influence of the hierarchies, zodiacal influence, and so on and so forth.

Threefold Being

The three functional subsystems of nervous, rhythm, and metabolic-limb pervade the whole organization of man. Through each system’s “center of gravity” so to say, each encourages a relationship to consciousness and spiritual influence. Firstly is the head organization. The nerve-sense subsystem has its center of gravity in the “head” and is the most spiritualized of the three systems because, ironically, it is the most physical and the one most subject to death. It is the oldest evolutionary inheritance, formed during Old Saturn, Sun, and Moon stages, and is therefore the “dying” part of the human organism. It’s physical foundation is the nervous system, which Steiner describes as the “lyre of Apollo”- an instrument through which cosmic harmonies are translated into individual soul experience. Furthermore, it is also the bearer of thinking and conceptual life. The distinctive feature is an operation through death. What this means is that the brain requires a continuous process of mineral deposition and subtle decay to serve as the instrument of thought. The nervous system constantly “dies” in order to create the space for spirit to enter. The head is emptier than empty, a bony vault which allows spiritual rays to penetrate directly from the cosmos, unmediated by the rest of the organism. In terms of the human constitution, the nervous system is essentially physical body only. The etheric and astral bodies recede here, allowing the “I” to experience the external world with the greatest degree of separation and objectivity. This is why thinking can achieve the clarity and detachment characteristic of conceptual life.

The rhythmic subsystem centers in the chest and consists of the breathing rhythm, blood circulation, and the circulation of other fluids like lymph and chyle. It is the mediating figure between the head and the metabolic-limb system. Evolutionarily, it corresponds to the Old Sun stage and is the bearer of the life of feeling. Two primary rhythms entwine here. First is the breath (approximately 18 cycles per minute) and the pulse (approximately 72 beats per minute), maintaining a ratio of roughly 1:4. The Greek hexameter, the four pulse beats structured within two breaths, producing the characteristic “long, short, short” pattern, was understood as an artistic expression of this inner harmony. All genuine poetry and music is thus ground in the rhythmic system. Unlike the nervous system, the rhythmic system is not primarily physical. It is the domain where the etheric and astral bodies work in balance, functioning as a symbolic diaphragm and holding apart the telluric forces of the upper human being (that is to say, the tendency toward mineralization) from the cosmic forces of the lower human being (tendency towards animalization). Because of this, the rhythmic system is the seat of health. Any time rhythm is disturbed, illness ensues. It operates with a dreamlike awareness. Certainly lower definition than thinking, but more fluid and interpenetrating, which is the very character of feeling.

The metabolic-limb system is the newest evolutionary development, belonging to the Earth stage of planetary development, and looking toward the future planetary stages (Jupiter, Venus, and Vulcan). It is the “coming-into-being” part of the human organism. The most spiritual in potential, yet in its current manifestation, the most bound to earthly substance. It is the bearer of will and has a unique relationship to physical substance. What appears as the metabolic-limb system is in actuality deposit. It is substance that is continuously being excreted or shed. The real metabolic-limb system is spiritual. The physical arms, legs, and organs of digestion are the sediment left behind by the activity of the will. We can think of this in a few ways. Firstly, let us consider the “hands”. No other animal in the world, despite having fingers and opposable thumbs, can make use of the hands in order to tinker with and manipulate the world how they see fit. We may also think of the example of activities which brings the soul delight. When one reads a book which broadens and enlivens the inner life of the soul, it is in essence a kind of digestion for the spirit. This is why will operates largely below the threshold of consciousness (namely sleep), its true throne is in the spiritual forces that constantly build up and tear down the body. The metabolic-limb is also where all four principles of physical, astral, etheric, and “I” work the most intensely, while at the same time where the “I” leaves only an imprint. The will, which this system serves, is the most future oriented faculty, the one through which the human being shapes their own destiny across future incarnations.

The Threefold Soul and I

A distinction must be made between the threefold soul and the bodies of astral, etheric, and physical, or what we may call bodily principles. The bodily principles can be said to describe the “structure” and organic condition by which the threefold soul’s inner activity and “I” can express themselves. Roughly, one can say the “I” lives within the bodily principles, and expresses itself through the threefold soul. They align as so: the sentient soul uses the astral body, the intellectual soul uses the etheric, and the consciousness soul uses the physical. The “I” itself is active in all three, although its presence becomes increasingly conscious as we “ascend” from sentient to intellectual to consciousness.

The sentient soul is the lowest member of the inner human being. It is the seat of the passions, desires, antipathy, instinctive reactions, sympathy, etc. The “I” broods dully here, hardly aware of itself. It receives impressions directly from the world and responds with attraction or aversion. It is also the most ancient soul layer, connected with the astral body and with the formative forces of sound (Mars forces). The astral bodily principle (or astral body) is the structure of sensation, desire, instinct, feeling, etc., as previously mentioned. It corresponds to the element of air, since it is stimulated mainly by breathing, which draws the airy element into the organism and carries it to the head in an attenuated state. In waking life, the astral body interpenetrates the physical and etheric bodies. Its shape is said to be egg-like, surrounding the physical body like a luminous cloud, extending about 2.5 times the head’s length beyond it. Its colors vary according to individual characteristics, with a dark violet spot in the region of the brain. It also has three energy centers being the brain, liver, and heart, which in the spiritual world form a six pointed figure, and in the astral world a triangle. Worth noting is that the astral body has a dual nature as a result of its evolution, consisting of a lower part inherited from the Old Moon stage, and the higher part which merged with the lower during the Lemurian epoch. Unlike the physical body, which exists in space, the astral body primarily exists in time. Steiner explains that the astral body does not fully descend into earthly life, instead opting to remain in the spiritual world, “throwing its beam” into the physical. When we observe a person’s astral body, we are actually looking into the spiritual world and perceiving what that individual underwent before birth. The human being is thus “like a comet stretching its tail far back into the past”.

During sleep, the astral body flies out of the others, in a sense “de-syncing” from the others. This is what enables feeling impressions upon the body when dreaming.
