When observed clairvoyantly, it appears as two intertwining spirals. One connects to the body, and the other extends far into the cosmos. Also occurring during sleep the astral body is “poured out over the whole of our solar system,” uniting with planetary beings and absorbing strengthening forces. It is from this cosmic union that the term “astral” (from astrum, star) derives. Regarding health, the astral body continuously produces arsenic within itself. This inner production is essential for consciousness and feeling. If the astral body is too weak in its connection to the organs, the etheric body proliferates, leading to conditions like diarrhea. If the astral body is too strong, it “arsenizes” too intensely, causing a tendency toward mummification and sclerosis. According to Steiner, therapeutic use of minute amounts of arsenic can strengthen an astral body that is too weak, although take this with a grain of caution. Additionally, the use of magnesium can counter an overactive astral body.

The astral world (or soul land) is the environment of the Earth corresponding to the element air, interpenetrating the physical world, in the same way that the human astral body interpenetrates the physical body. The astral world is populated by a hierarchy of beings, known in ancient Persian teachings as Amshaspands (beings with human bodies and animal heads, working as regents over forces of nature), Izards (beings of varied forms which assist the previous group in daily shifts), and Farohards (an innumerable amount of beings with forms combining plants and fish bodies with human heads, populating the astral world in countless variety). There are in effect two astral worlds: a higher “good” one and a lower “evil” one, both with laws operating in fundamentally different ways than the physical. For example, beings can occupy the same space; there is no physical impenetrability. There is also a unity of thought and being such that opinions and ideas cannot be debated and stored as thoughts, these ideas become deed immediately. When two opposing intentions meet, they must fight, and the more fruitful one annihilates the lesser. This is why tolerance is natural to the astral world. Access to the higher astral world requires renunciation. In ordinary experience after death (or initiation), a person encounters the images of their unfulfilled desires through painful symbology or frightful beasts and such. A transformation of these images occurs when one develops the capacity for renunciation in life. We may be getting too far out of the bounds of this article, so let us return to the threefold soul.

The intellectual soul (sometimes called the mind soul or emotional soul) is the middle, mediating member. It is where the “I” becomes higher definition, emerging to form thoughts, judgments, and concepts about the world in a clearer way. Operationally, it transforms sense impressions into an ordered experience, or inner life. The intellectual soul is aligned with the etheric body and is associated with light forces (Mercury). As the seat of courage, it holds the capacity for inner pictures and the beginning of self-reflection.

The consciousness soul (or spiritual soul) is the highest member. The “I” becomes fully self-aware. It is through the consciousness soul that man comes to know more than the physical and immediate world. We begin to know the spiritual world. Steiner states: “The consciousness-soul is in touch with the self-existent truth which is independent of all antipathy and sympathy”. The consciousness soul uses the physical body as its instrument and is associated with electrical forces (Jupiter).

So what relation does the “I” have to the threefold soul? The “I” is the concept of the human being, it is the centrality by which the peripheral and “subordinated” (hesitate to use this term since this might imply the wrong kind of relationship) threefold soul follows. It does not reside in any one soul member, instead residing in all three, progressively mastering them across incarnations, across the stages of a single life, or if you don’t make it in this round of being, in future developmental stages (and if you really don’t make it in the whole “cycle”, then you’re fucked for a while). Steiner uses a remarkable image. The “I” “plays on the instrument of the soul and the different strings of human soul life”. What emerges from this “playing” is what we call human character. This relationship is developmental. In early life, the “I” is most active in the sentient soul, working through instincts and impulses. As the child grows, the “I” increasingly takes hold of the intellectual soul, learning to form judgments and moral feelings. In full adulthood (around year 35. Remember that Jesus was baptized/initiated at 33), the “I” can fully awaken in the consciousness soul, attaining self-consciousness and the capacity for spiritual perception.

Importantly, the “I” never abandons the lower soul members as it transcends, it transforms them. When the “I” masters the intellectual soul, the sentient soul is elevated and refined. This may be reformulated as the power of thinking/feeling/willing over immediate instincts; mind over matter. It then follows that when the consciousness soul is awakened, it fructifies both the intellectual and sentient souls. This is why a truly mature human being does not lose passion, nor does not lose feeling, but transforms and spiritualizes them respectively.

Note: It must also be said that each soul member (sentient, intellectual, consciousness) expresses itself through all three functional biological systems, just as each functional system serves all three soul activities. The alignments mentioned only go as far as to indicate the bias (for lack of better term) of each soul member to system.

Human character is the expression of how the “I” “plays” upon the threefold soul. Recall the metaphor Steiner uses of music. Three factors shape this music. First, the innate disposition brought from previous incarnations, or the “definite attributes and characteristics” the “I” carries with it across lives. Next, the quality of the bodily principles, which involves a bit of spiritual economy re; karma, as well as care in this life. The physical, etheric, and astral bodies inherited may come one way or another, but they must also be maintained in this life. Following the music metaphor, a great pianist cannot play well on a broken piano. Lastly, the “I”’s own development plays a part, that is, the degree to which it has awakened in each soul member through conscious effort, spiritual practice, life experience, and so on. Two sculptures are highlighted in demonstrating these dynamics. The Laocoön group depicts a moment when suffering drives the human ‘I’ out of the body. The limbs writhe independently, no longer unified by a central “I”. Laocoön represents the fragmentation of the soul. The Niobe group shows the opposite condition, where the physical and etheric bodies become quite rigid under external attack/determination, freezing into form without the animating presence of the “I”. These sculptures reveal that the true character is neither fully chaotic fragmentation, nor rigidity.

Understanding the threefold soul provides us a practical framework for self-development, both spiritual and physical. The more apt among you might have noticed I have given a tacit justification for going to the gym and exercising in the previous paragraph. But we can make this prescription even more universal. We begin by observing the interplay of the three soul members in daily life. Be conscious of when you are acting from mere sentience (impulse, desire), from intellectual soul (judgment, feeling), or from consciousness soul (clear self-awareness, moral intuition). What do you notice? Is there a partiality? The goal here is to not suppress any member, but to strengthen the “I” as the unifying center which commands them harmoniously. One-sided development, such as an overly strong sentient soul without intellectual balance, produces the fragmentation symbolized by the Laocoön. Such work also requires caring for the bodily principles. The sentient soul needs a healthy astral body, the intellectual a spry etheric body, and the consciousness soul a well-ordered physical body (particularly the nervous system). Cultivating the consciousness soul would look like specifically practicing independent thinking, developing moral imagination, and nurturing a particular kind of reverence. One which does not require subservience. It also involves recognizing that this soul member does not mature fully until the 35th year or so. There is also the karmic dimension, this is not to be forgotten. The character we bring into this life is the result of work in previous incarnations, meaning what we develop now shapes future ones, giving self-development a non-immediate significance. It sanctifies daily efforts into an enduring spiritual substance.

Rhythms of Life

The “I” does not arrive fully transformed, not in this life, nor fully in this development. That which we call the “I”, that centrality, does not spring forth armed and masterful but slowly comes to know what it inhabits and manages. This growth follows a rhythm, unfolding across the years of a life in predictable cycles. Freedom is the fruit of this development. The “I” becomes free only by consciously taking hold of each stage and mastering what it offers. For now, we will only consider the current stage of human development with a consideration of what the future holds at the end. The following section traces the following rhythms: seven-year cycles, the 18-year rhythm, and the overarching arc of human life.

The most familiar pattern of development is the septennium, which marks the gradual incarnation of the “I” into the bodily principles. At birth, only the physical body is fully “born”; the etheric body is born around the seventh year (near the change of teeth), the astral body around the 14th year (puberty), and the sentient soul begins its independent activity around the 21st year. The intellectual soul follows around the 28th year, and the consciousness soul reaches its full awakening around the 35th year. Of note to the misguided materialist is that the biological milestones here will be mistaken as the determining factor (such as the full development of the brain being attributed to the emergence of the sentient soul). The relationship is quite the opposite. The spiritual guides the material. It is only by virtue of the concept of human being (this particular stage) that these events happen when they do. When confronted with finding a materialist explanation for why puberty happens when it does, the materialist is hard-pressed, often retorting some tautology of evolution (check out other articles on evolution).

Note: The reason most “reincarnation” cases have such prominence in early ages (e.g., children reporting past life memories) is precisely because the only bodily principle settled in the newborn baby is the physical body.

From birth to age seven, the child is a being of imitation, absorbing the environment through the physical senses. From seven to 14, the child lives in a natural relationship of authority; the healthy development of this period requires figures worthy of reverence, as the capacity formed here becomes the foundation for later equality and social responsibility. From 14 to 21, the young person awakens to independent judgment and the capacity for love, shaped by ideals encountered during these years. This is why childhood development is of utmost importance in the generation and reproduction of peoples. Moreso, from the ages of seven to 14, children want authority, they often look forward to structure imposed on them, mirroring (among other things) the astral body’s structuring quality of sensation. A desire for clarity and grounding due to tangential exposure to the astral world demands itself.

A second, longer rhythm runs parallel to the seven-year cycles. The 18-year rhythm, tied to the moon’s nodal cycle (~18 years and 7 months), corresponds to the nutation of the Earth’s axis and the moon’s return to the same position relative to the sun and fixed stars as at one’s birth. At these intervals - roughly ages 18–19, 37–38, 55–56, and 74–75 - a “spiritual window” opens. During these periods, the higher self (the “I” which descended at birth) may renew its impulses for the incarnation. Biographies of figures like Goethe, Schubert, and Swedenborg show pronounced creative crises or breakthroughs at these ages.

The 35th year is highly important for reasons already mentioned. Up to this age, the human being is occupied with building up the physical, etheric, and astral sheaths. After 35, these sheaths begin to recede. The forces that previously built the body become available for spiritual work, and wisdom becomes possible. In ancient Mystery schools, one was not permitted to teach before this age. Judgment given earlier was understood to belong more to the world than to the individual. After 35, the “I” can begin to give back to the world what it has received.

In the second half of life, development continues but becomes much more individual. The etheric body begins recession around age 42, and the astral body earlier. What can be called shadowy metamorphoses of soul occur around the end of the forties, again in the mid-fifties, and at the end of the fifties. These roughly correspond to what the human race as a whole experienced in earlier historical epochs. Old age recapitulates, in rudimentary form, the developmental stages of ancient humanity, just as during development from a young age, the child goes through the development of all ages of humanity before it.

Throughout life, these rhythms find resonance with each other. It is worth noting that these are not hardline rules, but more general tendencies of development. There is normativity, but let normativity have flexibility to it.

Final note: Humanity is getting younger.

One of the most counterintuitive findings of spiritual science is that humanity as a whole is not aging as an individual, it is growing younger. Perhaps the words of Christ to become like little children is fitting (don’t throw the tomatoes, no more jokes). The mainstream materialistic view assumes that ancient peoples thought and felt much as we do today, only with less scientific knowledge. This is extremely wrong on multiple levels. The relationship between the physical body and the soul/spiritual being has shifted drastically across cultural epochs, and this shift lets us know something regarding human freedom and the future of civilization.

In every human life, there comes a point when the physical body reaches its peak of growth and beings its slow decline. In ancient times, the soul and spirit remained dependent on the physical body for their development far into old age. A person in his fifties or sixties could still undergo significant inner transformation simply through the natural forces of the body. As the physical body declined, the soul blossomed. The patriarchs of old were not merely old in years; they were old in wisdom precisely because the body’s decline brought forth spiritual fruit. But this process has been shifting. Across the post-Atlantean [cultural] epochs, the age at which the soul becomes independent of the body has been steadily decreasing. It makes sense to think of it this way: spirit is becoming stronger and less dependent on the enabling conditions which provided its freedom. These conditions are being sublimated as spirit develops.

At the start of the Greco-Latin age, a person could still develop naturally into his mid-thirties. However by the time of the Mystery of Golgotha, humanity’s “age” had receded to approximately 33 years. Christ Jesus lived to the age of 33, meeting humanity at the very point where its natural development was faltering. Without the Christ Impulse, man would have been unable to access the spiritual world after this age threshold passed. Today, in the fifth post-Atlantean age (the age of the consciousness soul), natural development carries the soul only until approximately the 27th year. After that, the physical body continues to age, but the soul and spirit do not naturally develop further. Key word on naturally. They do not develop as a result of mere being-there. A person may live to be eighty, but unless he takes conscious initiative, his inner being remains at the age of 27.

There is a spiritual-physical basis for this. As the physical body grows older, the etheric body (the body of formative forces) does the opposite. It is old at birth and grows progressively younger throughout life. In ancient times, the declining physical body naturally released the etheric body, allowing spiritual experiences to arise in old age. In modern day, this release no longer happens automatically, meaning the etheric body still grows younger, but its youthful forces remain bound unless consciously cultivated. This is why a person can have gray hair and wrinkles, yet possess a soul that is becoming more flexible and more creative, more “young”. Spiritual science itself acts as a kind of rejuvenating force. One can study it at any age and find that it continually offers new insights, keeping the soul plastic and receptive.

So what are the implications of modern humanity? Firstly it is the end of natural wisdom. In older times, old age brought wisdom automatically. Today, wisdom must be actively sought out. One cannot simply rely on growing old to become wise. One needs to take inner development in hand. Another thing to consider is that since natural development ceases in the late twenties, young people often feel prematurely “finished”. The demand to lower the age of majority, to thrust the young into public life before they have truly matured, reflects this shift. If left unchecked, this tendency could lead to a society governed by the views of youth alone, without the counterbalance of elder wisdom. Although in America we are kind of stuck behind a rock and a hard place: geriatric Israeli worshiping pedophiles, or gen-z/gen-alpha brain rot. Another thing to keep an eye out for is the danger of spiritual stagnation. A person who does not consciously develop after the age of 27 carries the same inner configuration for the rest of life. Steiner warns that if humanity does not take up spiritual impulses, the consequence will be a form of spiritual schizophrenia. There is a disconnect between the physical age of the body, and the arrested development of the soul. Previously, my diagnosis for the problem of “man-children” was much more societal, that we had no “coming of age” ceremonies in the US. However, the problem is much deeper. Steiner gives us a couple of personalities as an example. Woodrow Wilson, although old on the outside, remind inwardly at the age of 27. Lloyd George also exhibited the same trait. He was brilliant and effective, but operating entirely within the horizon of what can be called the “27-year-old soul”. Conversely, people like Goethe or Schelling retained the capacity for inner transformation into later life because they drew from spiritual wellsprings. Goethe’s concept of metamorphosis and his continual self-revision highlight the consciously developed soul that grows younger, even as the body ages.

Ultimately, an emphasis on the necessity of conscious self-cultivation should be promoted. The waning of natural development is a challenge, one that makes sense as a stage of development, forcing man to take freedom seriously. What was once given by nature must now be won over through conscious effort. This serves as another reason why spiritual science is a necessity for the future, providing the means to continue developing the soul after natural development has ceased. If this trend continues, the age of natural development will continue to recede. In the sixth cultural age, it will fall to between 14 and 21 years. In the seventh millennium, it will drop to the 14th year. At that point, physical humanity will no longer be able to develop. Women will cease to bear children. The physical human race as we know it will come to an end. Supposedly. The main thing to keep in mind is that the “younger” humanity becomes physically, the more it must mature spiritually through its own free will.

item.title: Time Travel is a Christian Prospect
item.description:
Intro

When “time travel” is mentioned, the snarky retort of “we are travelling through time” often follows. What is meant by this is that by the very concept of being in time means that we are subject to the flow of time; to the power of Kronos. To state this only is to state the obvious. Of course when a prisoner travels from his cell to the yard, no one calls him a “traveler”. Yet he travels. Mere subjection to time, that fact of endurance in nature, is not what one means when time travel is evoked. The kind of time travel that sparks our imagination, the kind that carries spiritual significance and weight, is a question about agency, the degree to which one can step outside the determinations of nature, move or rebel against it, or perceive what lies beyond its power. Insofar as time travel exists, it must be of this latter sort, closely occupied with the question of freedom and agency. The aim here is to demonstrate what time travel actually is and its possibility.

To get a better grasp of what is meant, we may look to examples in fiction. When popular culture imagines time travel, it tends to reach for a very mechanical sense of the process, say the flux capacitor in the DeLorean, or the “bigger on the inside” spatially of the Tardis. The assumption shared by these icons is that time is another spatial dimension one can navigate with the right machine, a mechanical solution to a mechanical setting. One simply points a machine in the right “temporal direction” and moves. The appeal here is the promise of change without really doing anything. You remain yourself, only relocated to 1955, or the dawn of the universe. However, this materialist fantasy fails in that it mistakes the mere function of displacement in time for freedom, assuming that agency over time is a matter of engineering instead of a metaphysical endeavor. The machine does not change who you are, only changing where and when you are. Similarly to the prisoner, he may walk to the guard’s desk, the yard, to his cell, or to the cafeteria, but he remains a prisoner.

A more clever exploration of temporal agency appears in H. G. Wells’ The Time Machine. In this story, the Time Traveler constructs a framework for asking what time travel means and implies for man. His machine is quite selective and purposeful, but the story’s point is concerned with what one finds at the journey’s end. The traveler arrives in the year 802,701 to discover a humanity bifurcated into the Eloi and the Morlocks, as a projection of Wells’ anxiety about class division and the implications of the industrial revolution. His agency in time refuses the cheap “theme park-ification” of the past, opting for the revelation that time itself carries consequences. What Wells captures, among other themes and truths, is a thing which the materialist obscures in the form of a red herring. Genuine agency as related to time is never about locomotion, but about relation, something more ideal. Relation to the past, the future, and eternal now, and to the structures of power that would either reduce us to unfree beings, or become conducive to our budding sense of freedom. To understand time travel, we must first clear up what time is. The materialist treats time as a kind of neutral container, an additional fourth dimension alongside the three of space, assuming that space and time are commensurable. In other words, that the same logic governing extension in space governs the indifferent succession of time. It does not. Space, in its indifferent differentiation to point, line, and plane, presents us with a manifold where every dimension can substitute for another without altering the whole. Time, by contrast, has a for-itself difference. Its moments are not interchangeable in the same way. This may help to explain the resulting paradoxes, such as the “grandfather paradox”, and contradictions which result in this kind of conception.

Perhaps a metaphor will be useful here. Imagine an individual walking the perimeter of a circle on the ground, and trying to find the cause of the circle in the motion of the ciruclar movement itself. The circle’s motion (here the walking), is the phenomena itself, rather than the origin. So displacement in time, as if “displacement” is comparable to spatial displacement, never really has a conclusion. Additionally, the walker themselves never escape the motion of the circle as the symptom of a mistaken premise; that is, the misunderstanding of temporality leads to an enclosed circularity, never to be escaped because it never ended and never began. In other words, grandfather paradoxes and other similar logical indeterminacies are circular movements in space, simply transposed a pseudo “dimension” (that is, time as a misconceived extension of spatiality) higher into time.

A deeper consideration reveals to us that time as we experience it with waking consciousness is not a true spiritual reality, it is a material one, and thus a perspectival image, a Maya which serves its purpose. Just as we instinctively transpose three-dimensional space onto a two-dimensional surface and learn to read perspective in space, so too must we learn to see the perspective in time, that is, to place every being at its correct point in time, recognizing that what appears causally sequentially in the present may, in truth, be the working of spiritual forces. Time itself, far from being a neutral dimension, originates in the collaboration of the spiritual hierarchies. It came into being through the sacrificial deed of higher spirits, giving birth to the spirits (or concept) of time known as Archai. To speak of time travel is not to speak of navigating a spatialized fourth dimension. It involves relating consciously to the spiritual substance of time itself, particularly to the Time-Spirits who construct and guide the epochs, and to the Father’s power which underlies all nature as its eternal ground.

What time is it?

If we are to speak of time travel, we must first dispense the notion mentioned that time is a neutral contained. Time is not space. The moments of time do not coexist. They negate one another. The past is not, yet it was, the future is not, yet it will be, and the present is the only vanishing point between the two. The essence of time is negativity posited for itself, the “negative unity of being outside of itself”. It is, in Hegel’s formulation, the Kronos who gives birth to everything and destroys his offspring. This is the Kronos of ancient myth; the devouring father and the power that generates only to consume, it is that inexorable force which subordinates everything finite to its own transience. Kronos is the abstract negativity that underlies all temporal existence. What spiritual science adds to this picture is the recognition that this abstract negativity (rather, time as we experience it in waking consciousness) is not a given. It was created for a purpose. Time came into being through the work of spiritual beings who structure and order the conditions of succession and casualty. In effect, time is for spirit.

The Kronos of the philosopher successfully achieves the appearance of time within the sphere of nature. It is really the power of the Father, insofar as the Father is encountered as abstract law. The power that determines figurations as regular and establishes the relationships that science seeks to know is the work of the Father. But this is the Father in His exterior aspect. That power that holds nature in its lawful order, the Kronos who binds all things to the cycle of generation and decay. Yet time itself is not merely this devouring power. The concept of time is eternal. As Hegel himself says, “for it is neither just any time, nor the moment now, but time as time is its concept”, and this concept, in its self-identity is absolute negativity and freedom. Spiritual science gives the insight which Absolute Idealism provides a concrete spiritual content. The Archai, that is the Time-Spirits, are the true ancient time as the children of the Cherubim and Thrones. Time is the activity of these beings. The abstract Kronos which appears to us as the relentless flow of moments is the externalized image of the spiritual process constituted by the sacrificial giving and receiving which constitutes the cosmic order.

What this means for our inquiry is that time is truthfully a medium of relationship between the hierarchies, particularly between the Father and creation, and ultimately between the human soul and the eternal. Divisions of past, present, and future are certainly real, in a perspectival, relative sense (not to be confused with the “relativity” of Einstein, which no son or daughter of mine will support). They are forms through which we, in our state of separation from the spiritual world, experience the operations of beings who themselves dwell in a more unified relationship to time (or the time-spirits).

The question of time travel asks of man through spiritual means to relate to time as the time-spirits do. Can one learn to place every being at its correct point in time (in effect, thinking as the Time-Spirits do), while recognizing that on a higher register, linear succession is an assistant? To do this, we must then venture forth from what time is to its higher ideality in how it actually works as a field of spiritual agency.

Father time and the spirits

The reign of Kronos, the Titan of linear implacable time, is our subjection to the generation and decay of nature. Much can be said on the significance of Zeus slaying him and taking up the filled slot of Father. On the other hand, we have the qualitative reality of time as the workings of spiritual beings. They construct the epochs and hold open the possibility of development, redemption, history, and so on. These two powers are not identical, but are closely related, for both find their ultimate ground in the Father, whose nature is the substance of time itself. Everything in nature is subject to time, everything in time is subject to the Father. It’s almost a trope how mystics and those with experiential knowledge of spiritual worlds speak of the invalidity of spatio-temporality as we know it. For example, in the astral world, two beings can occupy the same space simultaneously. More has been said on this in another article.

In any case, the mythological imagination of Kronos is the father who devours his own children. This image conveys exactly the philosophical concept earlier- the pure abstraction of becoming that gives birth to everything, only to consume it once again. In this aspect, time is the manifestation of the Father’s power in its most external form. This is the Father encountered as gravity, entropy, causality, force, and such. In the natural order’s indifference to individual destiny, this relentless submission to sequence is the Father’s work. However, this is the Father as a kind of veiled figure, an abstract universal. Kronos knows no mercy, because he knows only the necessity of his own law. Modern science worships this power unknowingly. To be subject to this power implies an almost slave-like disposition, and likewise, the materialist conception of time travel, with its machine on machine manipulation, remains wholly within this dominion. It is a rebellion against the power of Kronos, while at the same time remaining subject to his ultimate sovereignty. Recall the prisoner metaphor; the materialist conception of time travel is almost like the prisoner mistaking the movement towards the yard for true freedom, that is, the freedom from something. It is doomed to be held apart from self-determination, or true freedom.

The Archai represent the internal spiritual reality of time, enlightening us on its origin and purpose. In their domain, in the domain of spiritual realms, the past is present as an influence, and the future is the “now” which occupies the present as the determinations spirit knows. They are beings who allow for spiritual development, for the possibility that what has been bound by Kronos can be loosened. They are the powers through whom history becomes the mark of the procedural development for freedom. This is all to say that the purpose of time, while being seen as restrictive conditioning, is in all ways necessary for the development of freedom.

Neither Kronos nor the Archai are ultimate. Freedom is ultimate. The “Father” (of which a distinction is to be made between Kronos, who is a father-figure, and the Father of the Christian Trinity) is the very subsistence of nature itself, echoing the Christian intuition that ““In Him we live and move and have our being” (Acts 17:28). It would be mistaken to view the Father as some distant legislator, setting the laws of nature in motion and then withdrawing, never to be seen again, as seen with the God of Deism. Space and time are emanations of His single, indivisible being, as one component (do not attack me for the usage here, how else should one refer to aspects of the Trinity?) of salvation. Our understanding of time is then transformed as a medium by which the Father speaks creation into being and sustains it. That is why Steiner says that “everywhere in nature is the Father”; He speaks to his creation through the mundane fact of being. To characterize Kronos as the Father misses the fact that He is not exhausted by law, He also comprises that medium of relation designated as the covenant between creator and created.

The possibility of “time travel”, and it’s meaning explored

So how can one “travel through time”? Even the phrase itself is extremely loaded. It suggests the kind of movement we have already done away with: a subject moving through a medium, as if it were a road, and we are just there along for the ride. If time isn’t this “container” idea explained before, but the activity of spiritual beings which create an environment conducive to freedom, and if its substance is not recognized as slavery, but an aspect of the Father’s reality He has given to us, then what we call “time travel” might be better described as “time relationship”. This would involve entering into relation with the powers that constitute time, and knowing, but not necessarily accepting, the forces which seek to bend temporal flow toward their own ends.

We have already spoken for the Archai, or the Time-Spirits, who do not move through time, but create it. Their relation to time is one of agency, specifically overseeing epochs and enabling the development of human freedom. Ahrimanic and Luciferic beings, like all spirits, do not dwell in the present as we do. Ahriman works from the past. His influence streams into the present from earlier ages, fixing things into rigid law and mechanism, promoting the illusion that what is determined cannot be changed. Lucifer, on the other hand, works from the future. He seduces one with possibilities unmoored from reality, from the now, pulling the soul toward premature spiritual heights that bypass process. Both seek to divert and distort the proper rhythm of time. Ahriman would freeze it into eternal repetition, Lucifer would dissolve it into formlessness. Neither has reverence for the Father, nor recognize it as necessary for the true development of man.

This all stands in stark contrast to modern fantasies of time travel. One of the more bizarre offspring of materialist thinking is “Roko’s Basilisk”, a thought experiment in which a future superintelligence punishes those who did not help bring it about, retroactively creating a motivation that reaches back through time. This is a parody of spiritual agency through an attempt to coerce the past by fear, reducing time to a deterministic chain that can be manipulated for a hypothetical future. What the Basilisk reveals, without knowing, is that the materialist framework necessarily must treat time as a thing to be exploited, since it has no place for love or freedom, the kind which binds moment to moment together. The Basilisk is in effect Kronos without the power of the Father, a purely devouring abstraction which only generates itself, much like the function of cancer.

We may also consider the human soul between death and a new birth. After death, the soul undergoes a reverse journey through the spheres, gradually shedding the earthly body and its attachments, memories, and such. Among the sharper minds reading this, a contradiction might present itself: if the soul is higher than the body, how then would it re-experience life? Would this not be like trying to fit an ocean into a single drop of water? The answer is quite simple. The higher “I” does not experience the sentient soul, or the part of the intellectual soul concerned with external sensory perception, only the members of the soul concerned with sense-free thinking, morality/moral ideas, art, etc., just as when the clairvoyant “thinks” as the elemental does (or perceives the spiritual being such that concept and percept are unified), they do not become the elemental. Similarly, the Archangel does not “see” these same aspects of the soul when looking at the human. However, the soul still experiences time in its perspectival reality. It looks back on the life just lived as a spatial panorama, and it prepares for a future incarnation by working with higher beings to shape the conditions of its next earthly life in accordance with it’s karma. Does man ever “reincarnate in the past”? Not in the sense of living an earthly life earlier than its previous one. Incarnation follows a forward succession because the Earth’s own evolution has direction. But the soul between death and rebirth moves in a realm where past and future are not as they appear to us as linear opposites. It participates in a domain where the rules of spatio-temporality do not apply, where it is reversible in influence, and oriented toward the redemption of what has been. The fact that reincarnation itself wasn’t always a thing should provide hints relating to the procedural affectations the soul undergoes.

It should also come as no surprise that certain forms of human perception can, under specific conditions, bypass the apparent constraints of spatiotemporality. Phenomena grouped under the names ESP and clairvoyance, such as remote viewing (which is a very sterilized form of clairvoyance for the purposes of military surveillance) and oneiromancy or prophetic dreams, all share a common feature. Space and time ceases to be a barrier. A remote viewer may describe a location thousands of miles away as if present, or an event from a distant century with the same immediacy as the morning’s breakfast. In these states, time does not matter since the perceiving soul has, for a moment, loosened its identification with the physical body and entered a mode of consciousness nearer to that of the astral world. One lives in time as much as one lives in space during this. A brief sidenote: the definition of death is the separation of the soul from the body, causing degeneration. While complete death is not possible, it would be irresponsible to suggest one engage in such activities. In fact, it may be the case that what is more conducive towards freedom involves sacrificing this capability in the name of God. Such activities are natural extensions of the perspectival nature of time. Importantly, these capacities do not grant mastery over Kronos. They are brief, fragmentary glimpses of what lies behind his immediate appearance to us.

Christian time travel

So far, even when we recognize the relationality of time, and that human souls already participate in temporal movements beyond the linear, we confront a limit. No being, whether human, animal, or “angelic”, can simply align itself with the Father’s perspective by its own power. Kronos holds us in his grip. The Archai, for all their wisdom, are not the Father, they serve His order but do not bridge the gap between His infinite perspective and our finite, temporal condition. In order to be drawn into the Father’s self-relation to time, that is, to stand outside of degeneration without ceasing to be the kind of being we are, another figure is required in the picture. We require a mediator who is both fully of the Father, and fully within time, one whose life, death, and resurrection open a way through the binding law of nature.

The eternal Word, the selfsame “through whom all things were made”, did not look upon His creation from the impassable summit of eternity. He entered it, taking flesh from birth, to hunger, to the slow accumulation of the years, to the stinging of crucifixion, and at last to the cold threshold of death. In this act, the Father’s perspective, which appears to us as Kronos’s grip, itself descends into the very sequence it ordained. Kronos, who seemed nothing but degeneration, came across Christ and his act of redemption. Christ was the one who took the past, including the sins and failures of humanity, into His own body on the cross and transformed it. The crucifixion became the axis, the fixed point by which the wheel of time turns. He is the Alpha and the Omega, the beginning and the end. In Him, the linear succession of moments is gathered into a single, presence.

When Christ says, “Do this in remembrance of me” (Luke 22:19), He is in effect instituting collapsing the conception of time which makes all things fade. We may take the Greek anamnesis as a word describing making something present. For instance, in the Eucharist the sacrifice of Calvary is represented as opposed to repeated. The offering becomes accessible to all, no matter age or congregation. This is the truest sense of “time travel”, a relationship with time involving spiritual participation in the eternal act, beyond the confines of spatiotemporality and the contingencies of nature. The Eucharist also reveals the relational nature of time as a feast, looking backwards to the cross, forward to the coming kingdom, and inward to the unity of the body of Christ. In the sacred meal, the faithful are lifted out of the degenerating current of Kronos and placed within the eternal time of the Father, mediated through the son.

The Archai, or Time-Spirits, oversee epochs and hold time open for the encroaching development of human freedom. Yet even they away fulfillment. Freedom itself requires a concrete source, and that source is Christ. In Him, the human “I” is united with the divine “I Am”. The Archai can be said to guide humanity through cultural ages. If this is the case, which it is, then Christ is the goal toward which those ages move. Hence, the Christian relationship to time participates in time by rightfully recognizing what time is. The believer, united to Christ shares in His resurrection, sitting with Him in heavenly places. From that vantage, the divisions of past, present, and future truly become relative. The Christian can pray for the dead with the assurance that in Christ, the boundary is permeable. He can intercede for the future with the confidence that his prayer shapes what is to come, and he can repent of the past with the certainty that all is forgiven and transformed.

“Time travel” is finally unveiled to us as the interaction with time that finds us becoming the kind of being who can dwell in the Father’s time. What has been explained thus far is the promise of theosis. Through union with Christ, the human person who comes to share in the divine mode of existence. To “travel through time” is to be drawn into the eternal movement of love between Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, a “movement” that generates time, and is not bound by what it makes. It is to stand with Christ at the wedding at Cana, and at the foot of the cross, and at the empty tomb, and at the feast of the Lamb, all in one eternal now, all in one indivisibility. This is the substance of faith, and the destiny of those who are in Christ. And it is offered freely, to all who will receive it, and to all who, receiving, cease to become arrested by time. This is the true meaning of “time travel”.

item.title: What is Natural?
item.description:
To speak truly, few adult persons can see nature. Most persons do not see the sun. At least they have a very superficial seeing. The sun illuminates only the eye of the man, but shines into the eye and the heart of the child. The lover of nature is he whose inward and outward senses are still truly adjusted to each other; who has retained the spirit of infancy even into the era of manhood. His intercourse with heaven and earth, becomes part of his daily food. In the presence of nature, a wild delight runs through the man, in spite of real sorrows. Nature says,—he is my creature, and maugre all his impertinent griefs, he shall be glad with me. Not the sun or the summer alone, but every hour and season yields its tribute of delight; for every hour and change corresponds to and authorizes a different state of the mind, from breathless noon to grimmest midnight. Nature is a setting that fits equally well a comic or a mourning piece. In good health, the air is a cordial of incredible virtue. Crossing a bare common, in snow puddles, at twilight, under a clouded sky, without having in my thoughts any occurrence of special good fortune, I have enjoyed a perfect exhilaration. I am glad to the brink of fear. In the woods too, a man casts off his years, as the snake his slough, and at what period soever of life, is always a child. In the woods, is perpetual youth. Within these plantations of God, a decorum and sanctity reign, a perennial festival is dressed, and the guest sees not how he should tire of them in a thousand years. In the woods, we return to reason and faith. There I feel that nothing can befall me in life,—no disgrace, no calamity, (leaving me my eyes,) which nature cannot repair. Standing on the bare ground,—my head bathed by the blithe air, and uplifted into infinite space,—all mean egotism vanishes. I become a transparent eye-ball; I am nothing; I see all; the currents of the Universal Being circulate through me; I am part or particle of God. The name of the nearest friend sounds then foreign and accidental: to be brothers, to be acquaintances,—master or servant, is then a trifle and a disturbance. I am the lover of uncontained and immortal beauty. In the wilderness, I find something more dear and connate than in streets or villages. In the tranquil landscape, and especially in the distant line of the horizon, man beholds somewhat as beautiful as his own nature.

The greatest delight which the fields and woods minister, is the suggestion of an occult relation between man and the vegetable. I am not alone and unacknowledged. They nod to me, and I to them. The waving of the boughs in the storm, is new to me and old. It takes me by surprise, and yet is not unknown. Its effect is like that of a higher thought or a better emotion coming over me, when I deemed I was thinking justly or doing right. Yet it is certain that the power to produce this delight, does not reside in nature, but in man, or in a harmony of both. It is necessary to use these pleasures with great temperance. For, nature is not always tricked in holiday attire, but the same scene which yesterday breathed perfume and glittered as for the frolic of the nymphs, is overspread with melancholy today. Nature always wears the colors of the spirit. To a man laboring under calamity, the heat of his own fire hath sadness in it. Then, there is a kind of contempt of the landscape felt by him who has just lost by death a dear friend. The sky is less grand as it shuts down over less worth in the population.

—Nature, Ralph Waldo Emerson

Nature Is An Imposition On Man

Hydra poison and angel tears, sifting through the veins of a lonely man. Nature, red in tooth and claw gnaws at the independent will, placing an obstacle before the suffrage due to him. Self-determination, the ability to act from a possessed principle instead of being pushed from the outside, requires an outside to push upon it. Nature is this imposition, a cold, material, temporal, and indifferent, yet highly beautiful constraint, which is essential for the emergence of genuine freedom. Without this imposition, self-determination would be a mere dream to be played in and with, in the face of any real accomplishment. Externality commences the first overcoming of freedom by establishing a context for organization- a rock lies where it lies and not where I will it, the river flows where it flows and not where I’d prefer it. The immediate being of nature confronts freedom with the possibility of alternation; in the quest for self-determination, things I did not determine exist. My body is in nature, and I do not immediately will it how I please. It imposes upon me hunger, fatigue, illness, and death. No act of freedom cancels the need to breathe or the fact of aging. Consequently, nature operates by blind and lawful regularity. A stone does not remain suspended in the air simply because I wish it. I must either work with or against gravity to have my will be done.

Consider all that imposes. Social custom claps a yoke upon the will, logic constrains our thinking, and our own habits and self-discipline limits from within. If Nature were but another such constable, another adder to the list of our limiters, her philosophical interest would be a thin and meager thing. The imposition of Nature is not like these at all. No, no. There is a separation all its own that sets it as far from social rule or logical bound as wide as the Grand Canyon. To know Nature, we must quit the shallow question of whether she restricts and swerve into the question of what kind of restriction is hers. The import of this change in questioning will influence whether nature is some alien to overcome, or perhaps a neutral resource to be used. And because the quality of the imposition changes what freedom means in relation to nature and vice versa, this second question is the more important one.

Uniqueness lies in the fact that nature’s imposition is at the same time impersonal and aesthetic. Unlike a tyrant or a social norm, nature does not act from malice or care. Floods wash away livelihoods and histories without hatred, and drought dehydrates all the same. This impersonality means no negotiation is possible; there is no bribery in falling from a great height, as if one can promise something to nature in exchange for arbitrarily halting her rightful law. In all cases, the one with the upper hand is nature. Yet the same impersonal forces often produce such breathtaking beauty in the world. The mountain’s sublimity or the flower’s delicate form, or the snowflakes’ symmetry- the combination of indifferent splendor is found nowhere else in human experience (barre natural disposition in spirit). Another uniqueness is in the fact that nature’s imposition is at the same time restrictive and generative. The very laws of nature that block one aspect are enablers of another as a result of indifference. While gravity may prevent one from floating, it also holds the atmosphere that lets one walk. A species’ morphological type limits variation, but that limit is what makes organized diversity possible. Without it, there is complete chaos and Ahriman’s imagination runs wild. Social and self-imposed rules typically function as negations imposed upon a subject, but nature’s negation is intrinsic to the very conditions that make the subject possible. The former places the onus on the subject to create alternation, and as such, the prohibition of speech, for instance, generates coded language and satire, but this generativity depends upon subjective receptivity and response. Within nature, a displacement of the negation occurs immediately and immanently within the domain of nature itself; the force of gravity’s forbidding of “free” movement, is what fosters the very field within which movement becomes possible in the first place. Nature’s imposition is then understood to be highly material. It has a lawful duality to it, as it works in and through bodies and energies. The constraints within nature leave very real traces that cannot be cancelled by thought alone. Unlike a mistaken belief, which can be corrected by reasoning, nature’s imposition demands action in the world. As such, the thinking of nature has immediate consequences: nature thinks herself into being, and if she is mistaken, then she dies. When the plant requiring sustenance is watered by an acidic substance, that is, ironically, nature’s own produce, then the plants being cannot be debated because its thought has failed. Because of this, nature becomes a great teacher of lawfulness in the world. The child learns to not touch the hot stove either by heeding the warning of adults or by the singing of their new flesh, and a fear of falling from tall heights stems from the impact of broken bones on concrete. Such an imposition is quite reliable.

Nature As Such

Any genuine inquiry into nature must begin with a singular and unwavering rule: look for the reason something happens within that thing itself, not in any outside source. Two common habits of thought are dispensed with in this approach. The first is the habit of saying that a natural object is designed according to a premade plan or telos (for example, saying that an eye is made the way it is because a “designer” wanted the eye to see). The second habit is a reductionism towards mechanism, as if a tree were no different from a pile of rocks simply because common forces of pushing, pulling, collision, and so on work upon them both. Both habits fail because they bring in a law from outside the object, rather than letting the object reveal its own inner lawfulness. What this means concretely is that this principle requires that when one studies a stone or plant, they refuse to ask “what externality determined this”, opting instead for the positionality that seeks what must be true within this thing to make it appear exactly as it does. The investigator’s vocation is to arrange the object or event (in this case nature) so that its own behavior becomes self-explanatory. No picture of the world, no matter how detailed a listing, yields comprehension. Comprehension arrives when the mind creates a fertile concept that makes sense of and coheres a set of percepts. Such a disciplined act of thinking stays entirely within what is given, and in one swift movement lifts the given into a higher ideality. For example, watching a lamp swing back and forth does not by itself teach the law of the pendulum. That law appears only when someone thinks the swinging motion as a relation between length and time, stripping all accidentals like air or imperfect suspension. Uncovering the intelligibility which was always there, but hidden behind the surface constitutes any real advance in knowledge. Otherwise, lacking this vocation results in a heap of unrelated facts.

Nurturing a worldview that carries its own justification is the goal of any true inquiry. It should not need to lean on outside supports such as an external, guiding divine plan, or notions like “common sense”. Explaining each part by its place in the relational whole and that whole is nothing more than an unfolding of its own starting points is what is known as self-sustaining. Without this, knowledge remains arbitrary, and our explanations risk the sin of overstepping their post. For instance if in an explanation of a living being we borrow principles from non-living matter, we have not understood the living being. If an inquiry into nature would embrace something such as a presumed external designer, it will have stepped outside nature altogether. Only when the explanation arises from within the thing itself have we achieved a conclusion that is both true to the object and satisfying to reason. Furthermore, immediate everyday experience is full of disorder and contingency. Things happen because of accidental circumstance: this stone fell here, not there; this leaf grew crooked because a bug bit into it at this spot, and so on and so forth. Raw reality often disappoints our deeper need for comprehension, and so inquiry should not be in accordance with this chaos, it should raise us to a higher kind of perception. In this way, inquiry may give us a world that is known.

Verily, nature is not a machine. A machine is assembled from the outside and follows a designed conceived from elsewhere, having no inner principle of self-organization or self-repair. Nature, by contrast, certainly does not arrive prepackaged from a foreign intelligence onto dead matter. Her laws are the very way she unfolds from within. No doubt, organisms exhibit an admirable ordering. That order need not be traced back to a planner standing outside nature, no reason connects this to reality beyond a convenience, or rather laziness, of thinking. To do so would also be to explain away the living by something quite dead, placing the source of intelligibility beyond the thing we seek to understand. Nature’s order is immanent on the first count.

As understood most comprehensively, nature is best described as the dynamic, self-organizing whole of spatiotemporal existence. Every thing in nature is a temporary expression of underlying powers beyond the natural world, but make their existence known in it. What these powers are is where the thinker plays a part, whether this thinking is done by the nature spirits who think rocks and stones, leaves and trees, and storms and hurricanes into being, or on part of the human subject seeking to connect with the world and broadly think as it does. Being itself characterized by expressing underlying powers, nature is not exempt from its reflexive self-characterization. Nature itself is a living process that conditions and participates in the emergence of spirit. This is the underlying power behind nature, a spirit in its own right which hosts the concretization of spirit in the world. Now, there is a more subtle denial of nature’s freedom that comes from turning nature into a waypoint for spirit. Some philosophies (most famously the grand systems that see all reality as the journey of spirit) treat nature as a foreign land that Spirit must begrudgingly pass through, suffer in, and ultimately abandon. In this view, nature is real only as a moment of self-estrangement. This should be rejected, not on the denial that spirits, angels, or even God having created nature for its pregnancy, this much should be acknowledged. It should be on the fact that creation is always meaningful. A painter who gives a painting its own inner light does not reduce the painting to a footnote of the painter, and likewise a composer who writes a fugue does not make each note a slave to the harmony, let alone the composer’s biography. So too, if nature is created, she may be created as a genuine other, as a real presence and domain with her own kind of truth. The positivism just described only serves to veil what nature herself veils, forbidding us from getting to the truth of the matter.

The Spiritual Intuition of Nature

At some point or another, most people have looked at a forest, a starry night sky, a newborn animal, or even in some cases a natural disaster, and felt something that goes beyond the physical fact of description, passed the sublimity of awe and reverence, and into the intuition that what is being looked at carries a sense of something alive in a way that a pile of bricks or a machine isn’t. Say we watch a spider rebuilding its web after a storm or a wounded animal limp to safety. Even something as “basic” as a seedling pushing through soil is truly anything but basic, and one senses something that purely chemical descriptions miss. The chemistry is not wrong, mechanics and chemistry play a part and are true on their own level. It is the phenomenon that exceeds the sum of these parts, a persistence implying a unity of some kind. Meaning the language of physics and chemistry applies on one level, to the degree that a seed responds to temperature and moisture and gravity, these are not false, but are insufficient. The seedling at the same time does not choose a direction, it grows toward light as if it knows where the sun is, presenting a self-maintenance in the face of the entropic principle. The ability to repair damage and coordinate the sometimes thousands of chemical and mechanical processes simultaneously are signatures of an inner impulse. Often it is said that life wants to continue, as if life itself had a will. In one sense this is a kind of anthropomorphism, in the sense that the same quality of life present in planets, the sun and moon, animals, and plants, is also present in people. However, this thing called “life” cannot truly be called anthropomorphic on this account since it is an honest report of how the phenomenon of life presents itself. There is an inwardness that even a single cell exhibits, a kind of intention without a brain. It cannot be considered a conscious intention, but it is certainly not random either. Goethe would call this the Bildungstrieb, or formative drive; Schelling saw nature’s productivity as the world soul’s first stirring. These names are not necessary, you only need to have held a living thing in your hands and felt that it is doing something. Nature’s intuition is doing something and not just happening. If the natural is alive in any kind of way, it is constituted in part by an excess of chemism and mechanism, which are so often unified as one deterministic explanatory power. In this case, the death of a lifelong family pet or the felling of an old tree feels like the loss of presence because it is. Nihilistic attitudes would accuse subjective feeling of experiencing lament over the loss of a mere function. We can only pray that the same indifference felt toward nature by such individuals is not mirrored by others as a fulfillment of their world picture.

There are places that feel different. One may be in an unremarkable forest in one instant and in the next step into a clearing of some kind and have the quality of air change. Perhaps the light seems older, and the silence becomes deafening. Places like these may be unmarked my human hands but carry a significance more impactful than religion. Truly, an encounter in nature may itself be a religious experience. The intuition here is that the land itself has a kind of character beyond geological oddities. Places that feel chosen somehow, that the Earth has arranged for itself a temple or holy site as integral of its own being. Across cultures, such places become sites of pilgrimage and ritual because people cannot ignore the feeling. “Ley lines” are a more structured attempt to name the various church sites, pyramids, alignments of ancient standing stones, wells, and natural features that seem to follow energy pathways invisible to the naked eye. Whether one believes in their literal existence or not, the underlying experience is real. Some paths across the land feel alive, as if you are walking along a current. Farmers speak of earth energies that affect plant growth and animal behavior. Even so-called skeptics admit that some places are uncannily calming or unsettling, although mechanical and chemical explanations will furnish their justifications in feeling.

The world itself undergoes repetition with difference. Spring brings with it a kind of return through the story of emergence and the break of the dormancy of winter. Autumn is a release of beauty and mourn. The tides breathe alongside the moon, they do not just blindly repeat the motion of water. Each phase transforms into the next while preserving something essential, and by this very fact alone, cycles not only feel but are larger than their physical causes. What Goethe calls the Urphänomen (primal phenomenon) tries to capture this sensory experience of a law that governs the entire domain of nature. Most famously, an example can be found in the metamorphosis of the plant. Looking at countless plants, he saw that all their parts (the petals, fruit, stamens, sepals, etc.) are in reality the same organ transformed. That organ is the leaf. This leaf expands, contracts, refines, and specializes. The cotyledon may be considered a leaf with stored food, and the stem leaf a leaf that breathes. The petal is a leaf having become delicate and bright, the stamen a leaf coiled to hold pollen, and the fruit a leaf swollen around seeds. The whole life of the land is the leaf’s journey thrush a series of gesturing. Such an archetype is present and visible right now in every [healthy] plant. All it takes to see it is training. Once one does, the plant ceases in being a collection of parts and transforms into a performance of a single theme, found in the visible idea of the plant’s growth cycle. Goethe called this the Urpflanze (primal plant), claiming that with it, we can speak a language of forms that would apply to all plants, even ones never before seen. This approach also works for other natural phenomenon, such as color. Goethe identified the primal phenomenon of color as the appearance of colored edges where light and dark meet through a turbid medium (e.g., the sky at sunset or a prism held against a black and white boundary). From this singularity, the universal world of colors could be derived. The cycle of light and dark, yellow and blue, and so on and so forth are the metamorphosis of the primal contrast. When one learns to see this, they learn to see themselves in nature and their own growth; you begin to see your own growth and phases of expansion and contraction, your unfolding and fading, etc. Goethe claimed this is why the true scientist is like a poet, insofar as both read the living archetypes of nature.

The most intimate and at the same time most easily dismissed report by people across all cultures, social classes, and eras, from hermits to children, to hunters and to gatherers, is the experience of not being alone in a natural place. A forest or a meadow in which one feels seen or accompanied without seeing anyone can surely be explained away by the skeptic tools of chemical-material reductionism. Yet what cannot be explained away is the feeling of inhuman presence as a quiet attention sourced from the land itself, or rather, from the beings that dwell in and constitute it. Some may call this the genius loci, or the spirit of a place. Others, like Steiner, name specific orders of undines in flowing water, sylphs in air, gnomes in stone and earth, and salamanders in fire. The names are less important than the sense that the feeling is different from forms of social recognition. When a person looks at you, you feel judged, or maybe ignored, but definitely acknowledged in some way. When a forest looks at you, it is without expectation. It simply notes you, as one would note a ripple in a pond. Artists and poets have always known this. Urban gardeners speak at times of their plants “liking” a particular spot or “preferring” a certain neighbor. For those who have felt it, it changes how they walk in the world.

Quite the strange feeling may come across oneself when standing in a forest after rain, or watching a thunderhead build over a plain. This feeling returns to us in our consideration of nature because it is an expression of the notion that *this world is older and larger than the ‘I’ which I know now, yet I am made of it”. Rising from that, “I am also not that”. The rock does not wonder, and the cloud does not ask “why”. But people do. This intuition in the face of nature is in essence the fact that nature invites us to sanction nature by recognizing ourselves as in and of nature, in part.

The stone feels utterly other. It is cold and hard, yet you can hold it. Its weight is the weight of the Earth. Your bones and teeth are similarly made of the minerals present in the Earth, those of calcium, magnesium, and other substances that come from the Earth, just as the stone is formed. In the stone, the “hardness” about it can come off as a sturdiness as deep as the Earth goes. Just as the stone holds place in the Earth, the mineral kingdom is the foundation of our upright posture, making up the skeleton that has a shape which penetrates the downward push of gravity and holding our form in place. The mineral realm seems farthest from us, but it is the foundation of us as beings in the natural world. Our kinship with the stone is that it provides the resistance without which our “I” could never become free. Our bones are mineralized, teeth crystals, and the iron in our blood was forged in stars and carried in rocks before it ever entered a living cell. How awe filling is it that our posture depends on the calcium and phosphorus that once slept in ancient seabeds? To know the mineral world in nature is to know the sacrificial descent of spiritual beings who condensed themselves into matter so that we may have a body. When holding a quartz crystal, one holds a thought of Ahriman that has become tangible and enduring. And because ones own thinking can think that thought, you may comprehend the same spiritual activity that gave the stone its form.

Plants share a different order of kinship. We certainly do not photosynthesize, nor do we send roots into soil. But our body on one level depends on plants via the oxygen they release, the fruits and seeds we eat, the shelters, medicines, and clothes we make from their fibers and resins, and so on and so forth. More intimately, the rhythm of our own growth (particularly as responsive to seasons and light) mirrors the plant’s way of being. A child’s body grows like a seedling, and elderly person’s body withers like an autumn stem. The plant encourages us to recognize that our own bodies, in their lawful, rhythm, self-forming activity, is a continuation of the same formative forces that shape the leaf and the flower. The plant itself lives in the etheric realm, and so do we, with the addition of an “I” that has the ability to reflect on that living.

Weather is different yet again. Storms are events constituted by passing intensities of wind and water and light. One cannot hold a storm, only be within it or avoid it. The storm has no character the way an animal or plant does, yet it has mood. A sudden thunderstorm feels like anger or release, and on the other hand, the slow, gray, drizzle feels like melancholy. The storm invites us to recognize that it and you are alike, but that you can be more than it, because while the same forces that move the clouds also move your own inner atmosphere, the storm is not conscious of it. The weather itself does not think, but you who uses the same air, can think about weather. Storms in the natural world eventually pass, even the Great Red Storm on Jupiter, and so too will furious storms of emotions in the soul. In modern times, perhaps all should be prescribed time to routinely listen to the wind. It is different every day.

Animals also stand at a different threshold, and have been considered thoroughly elsewhere. Because of the animal kingdom’s proximity in form and being in the natural world to man, they express to us our own potentialities taken to the extreme, only to be taken up into ourselves again. In all cases, the intuition is the same. Nature is our own unfinished being, shown back to you in forms that cannot complete themselves. Man completes them by understanding them, living with them in harmony, and using consciousness to act freely in areas where nature can’t.

A brief consideration on the heavenly bodies: These also hold a special kind of kinship, touched upon briefly elsewhere in this blog. There is an older intuition, still present, that the planets and stars are not only objects, but beings. This is the same kind of recognition you might have when you meet a person. You see their body, but you also sense that something lives within that body, something that is looking out through the eyes. The stars and planets also have a kind of gaze. Not a human gaze with a face, although “A Trip To The Moon” makes a compelling point, but a presence that is intelligent in a way differently than Earth-intelligence, in the most literal of terms, with influences on us that have not gone unnoticed throughout various cultures in time.

Of course none of this requires a belief in spirits or the hierarchies, only that you take your own body seriously as a part of nature. Once you do, the boundary between self and world begins to soften just a bit. The pain of the animal is sympathized with in part as a version of your own vulnerability, and the plant’s growth is your own metabolism slowed down, for example. The boundaries we draw between the kinds of being are useful for scientific purposes, but do not capture the deeper comprehension that the whole of nature is alive and related. The nature of this kinship differs radically across the kingdoms, but the relationality remains. Ultimately, the kinship one feels with nature is recognition of the fact that nature was never separate from spirit.

The Shortsightedness of Absolute Idealism on Nature [WIP SECTION - UNFINISHED]

If animals are indeed separated potentialities of the human form, they carry within themselves the seed of what we have become, and what we may potentially overcome. What appears in the animal as instinctive and one-sided, bound to the collective existence of its animal nature, is the same force that in us becomes conscious and free. Following from this, the animal kingdom is not a static gallery of evolutionary “failures” or any other such nonsense. It is the field of future possibilities. People already sense this when they observe a dog’s loyalty, or a horse’s sensitivity, a monkey’s human-like movement, a crow’s problem-solving capability, and so on and so forth. In the natural world, these capabilities are the shining-forth of a future where the animals as the field of future possibilities themselves evolve in accordance with what they are the future of.

The group souls that now direct the bee’s hive, the spider’s web, the bird’s migration, and the beaver’s dam will begin to individualize. That same spiritual activity we call magnetism and somnambulism will, in those future stages, belong to animals as their own domain. Surely, they will not become human, however they will become what animals can become when the human potential that was frozen in them is finally relinquished. This stands in stark contrast to the current understanding of Spirit and Nature in Absolute Idealism. Parapsychological or various other spiritual capabilities are shown to be distinctly designated in the domain of “Nature”, yet the reasonable thinking man, who is trained to see beyond immediacy, can see that these capacities are already present in the animal kingdom. One needs only look towards the explicit experiments of Rupert Sheldrake on animal telepathy. If not this, then the seeds already present in the animal kingdom: migrating animals and homing pigeons are not tricks of chemistry, as chemistry is subordinated to them. These are forces in the spiritual world acting through nature, preparing the animal for a time when it will act from within its own dawning inner life, as a reflection of the immense universality present within the human inner life. Kinship of this kind is prompted by nature at every step of the way, for those that have the eyes to see.

Much of philosophy has missed what the point of kinship with nature illuminates. Logicist Idealism, for all its sophistication, has often treated nature as something merely to overcome in the form of a logical stage to be “sublated”, as a mirror that reflects only what spirit already knows of itself. Even if nature supposedly does not immediately display the full richness of spirit, or in other words, if nature hides the processes one finds in what can be (for the purposes of argument) called the spiritual world proper, dismissing nature would be like ignoring a newborn baby because it cannot yet speak or show its potential.

Essentially, proponents of logicist idealism do have a seed of truth. Forgiving an enemy is more true to spirit than a rock’s being heavy. The issue of spirit following or preceding nature is not the problem. In fact, the spiritual world is responsible for nature, and however one wants to put the organization (whether it is God responsible for nature, which God is), the ontological map is not in contention. Even if nature does not constitute what angels are, nature is an enabling condition for spirit to convey itself. To not recognize that we are constituted by nature, and that nature exists objectively and has a certain kind of independence is a form of undermining ourselves. Any true [Christian] and/or ethical impulse would preserve nature out of love and gratitude. For the retards and intentionally obtuse, this does not mean that nature is on the same level as spirit, for the umpteenth time, just as magnanimity towards animals prevents rationality from concluding in an equation of animality and humanity.

How Do We Overcome Nature’s Imposition?

The whole of physical being is nature. Living and non-living, beautiful and deadly alike. We are part of nature because we are in the world. At the same time, we may step back and abstract ourselves from nature, only because this is the hallmark of thought- the beginnings of freedom in a being through the capacity of developing an inner life. Nature imposes on our wills, and we overcome that imposition by working within it and transforming it. Nature is the given, it is a rock that falls, the hunger that wears us down, that stinging cold, and the blistering heat above. It imposes on us because we are of it and live inside nature.

Thinking is the first and primary, some might say only way we overcome this imposition. An animal feels hunger and seeks food, meanwhile a human can feel hunger and decide to fast for this reason or the next. Such a decision comes from an inner life, in other words the ability to form an idea that overrides the immediacy of natural impulse. This development of inner life is the beginning of any freedom in a being. Moral intuition as the direct sense of what is right, as opposed to what is “natural”, is bound upon and grows from this same capacity. Thinking in us meets nature’s push with a response outside of nature, compelling this cunning outside-of-nature-ness to take control.

This “taking control” opposes the imposition of nature, which is the given. Culture is what the human being contributes in this act of taking control. It begins the moment man takes something natural and does something with it that nature alone would not do. For instance a stone is natural. But a stone shaped into a hand axe is cultural. A handful of clay is nature. That same clay shaped into a bowl and fired into a kiln is culture. A cry of pain is natural, and a poem about pain is culture. The “opposition” in question is not a diametric opposition to nature, in fact culture grows out of and uses nature, never really escaping its laws. But it does add human willing and form. And so a bird building a nest from twigs is natural, with the bird following instinct. Every nest of that species looks nearly the same. When a human builds a house, that house is cultural.
